paragraphs.en: Magna Carta basically did two things: It required the King to raise taxes by holding a Great Council and established the principle that no man could be arrested or fined unless he was first found guilty in court of law. Henry III died in November of 1272 and was succeeded by his son Edward I of England. Edward decided to use this idea of a great council to his advantage. In 1295 he called a Great Council with 2 representatives from every county. The Great Council authorized new taxes and in return they brought grievances and complaints to the King. The Great Council is known to history as the "Model Parliament"
paragraphs.zh: 本质上来说，《大宪章》做了两件 事：一是要求国王通过组建一个大 议会来提高税收；二是确立了除非 在法庭上被判有罪，否则不允许对 任何人进行逮捕或罚款的原则。 亨利三世于 1272 年 11 月去世，他 的儿子爱德华一世继承了王位。 爱德华决定利用大议会的名头谋取 私利。1295 年，他召集了一个由各 县出 2 名代表组成的大议会。 大议会同意了开征新税的请求，作 为回报，他们有权向国王提意见提 要求。 大议会在历史上被称为“模范议 会”，因为它为后来的英国议会制

paragraphs.en: because it established the system for what eventually became the English Parliament. Asystem developed whereby Parliament would approve taxes if the King would make reforms to his government. By 1625 a legal tradition in England had developed. First, no man could be imprisoned or fined by the government without a fair trial. Second, the King could not raise taxes without the agreement of Parliament. Charles did not like these restrictions on his powers.And so he got into a fight with his Parliaments over and over again from 1625 until 1629.
paragraphs.zh: 度奠定了根基。 如果国王对政府进行改革的话，那 么议会就会批准征税。 到了 1625 年，英格兰的法律传统已 经发展起来。 首先，未进行公平的审判之前，任 何人都不会被政府监禁或罚款。 其次，没有议会的同意，国王不能 增税。 查理不喜欢这种对他权力的限制。 1625—1629 年间，他一次又一次地 同议会斗争。

paragraphs.en: Finally, in 1629, Charles declared that he would never call another Parliament and would rule England without them. But without Parliament Charles couldn't raise any taxes so he was constantly broke. So he tried to raise money by giving companies rights to have monopolies, by forcing rich men to give him loans that he never paid back, by creating different types of fines, and by levying illegal taxes. This brought him just enough money to make ends meet, but also angered people all across England. Charles could have gotten away with all of this, but in 1637 he made a big mistake. He turned his attention North to Scotland, which
paragraphs.zh: 最后，在 1629 年，查理宣布不再组 建议会，并将在无议会的情况下统 治英格兰。 但没有议会，查理就无法增税，所 以他时常破产。 因此，他试图通过赋予公司垄断权 利、强迫富人给他贷款并且从不还 款、设立不同类型的罚款以及征收 非法税收等手段来筹集资金。 此举给他带来了足够的钱维持收支 平衡，但也激怒了全英国的人。 查理本可以逍遥法外，但 1637 年他 犯了一个大错误。那年，他把注意 力转向了北部的苏格兰，那里的国 教与英格兰的并不相同。

paragraphs.en: had a different national church from England. Charles wanted to force the Church of Scotland to become part of the Church of England. So on July 23rd 1637, Charles' bishops tried to get the Scots to use the English Book of Common Prayer at St. Giles Cathedral in Edinburgh Scotland. This angered the Presbyterian Scots who did not want the English to interfere with their church, so a riot broke out in the cathedral. The next year in 1638 the Scots organized their resistance by signing a document called the National Covenant that pledged to fight for the independence of the Scottish Presbyterian Church.
paragraphs.zh: 然而查理想强迫苏格兰教会变成英 格兰教会的一部分。 1637 年 7 月 23 日，查理的主教们 试图让苏格兰人在苏格兰爱丁堡的 圣吉尔斯大教堂使用英国的《公祷 文》。 这激怒了苏格兰长老会教徒，因为 他们不希望英国人干涉他们的教 会，于是教堂里爆发了骚乱。 第二年，1638 年，苏格兰人签署了 一份名为《国民盟约》的文件，表 示他们将为苏格兰长老会的独立而 战。

paragraphs.en: 1 out of every 3 Scottish men signed! And when it became clear that Charles wouldn't back down, the Scots raised an army, and by the summer of 1639 they had 12,000 men under arms in the north. So Charles decided to call the first Parliament in 10 years in order to raise money for an army. Although nobody knew it at the time, this Parliament would last until 1660. So it is known to English history as "The Long Parliament" Before Pride's Purge, Parliament had 470 members, afterwards they had only 210. This remnant of the original Long Parliament is known to English
paragraphs.zh: 每3个苏格兰人中就有1个签了名! 很显然，查理不会退缩，所以苏格 兰人招募了一支军队，到了 1639 年夏天，苏格兰军队在北方就组建 了一支 12000 人的军队。 于是查理决定时隔十多年重启议 会，为军队筹款。 虽然当时并没人知道这次的议会将 持续到 1660 年。所以英国历史上称 这一时期的议会为“长期议会”。 在普赖德清洗之前，议会有 470 名 成员，而在那之后只剩 210 名了。 原来的的长期议会的残余在英国历 史上被称为“残余议会”。

paragraphs.en: history as "The Rump Parliament" On January 1st, 1649 the Rump Parliament decided to put Charles I on trial. The trial lasted from January 20th to 27th And on January 27th the court found Charles guilty and sentenced him to death. This is his death warrant. Charles said goodbye to his two youngest children on January 29th. On January 30th he marched from his prison to the Banqueting House in the center of London. This is the banqueting house today. There a scaffolding had been prepared and a large crowd had
paragraphs.zh: 1649 年 1 月 1 日，残余议会决定对 查理一世进行审判。 审判从 1 月 20 日持续到了 27 日。 1 月 27 日，议会认定查理有罪，并 判处他死刑。这是他的死刑令。 1 月 29 日，查理和他最小的两个孩 子告别。 1 月 30 日，他从监狱游行示众至伦 敦市中心的国宴厅。 这是今天的国宴厅照片。 那里已经搭起了支架，人头攒动。

paragraphs.en: gathered. Charles was very calm and faced his death with dignity and courage. He pulled back his hair, knelt down, and gave the signal to the executioner. And on January 30th, 1649 he was beheaded. This is the first time in modern history that a democratic movement successfully overthrew a King. His body was taken to St. George's chapel by his friends and family. And he is buried there today. For the next 4 years the country was ruled by Oliver Cromwell and the Rump Parliament. But the Rump became corrupt and stopped doing anything useful or
paragraphs.zh: 查理非常平静，带着尊严和勇气面 对死亡。 他把头发往后一拢，跪下来，向刽 子手发了个信号。 1649 年 1 月 30 日，他被斩首。 这是现代历史上第一次民主运动成 功推翻国王。 他的遗体被朋友和家人带到了圣乔 治教堂。 他如今就葬在那里。 在接下来的 4 年里，国家由奥利 弗·克伦威尔和残余议会统治。 但残余势力变腐败了，他们不再做 任何有用的事情，也不选举。1653

paragraphs.en: holding elections. So on May 7th 1653, Oliver Cromwell dissolved the Rump Parliament. As he kicked them out of Parliament saying: "You have sat too long for any good you have been doing lately ... Depart, I say; and let us have done with you. In the name of God, go!" From 1653 until 1659 Cromwell ruled England directly as the "Lord Protector" This period in English history is known as "The Commonwealth" period. But then Cromwell died on September 3rd, 1659. And then Parliament returned to power.
paragraphs.zh: 年 5 月 7 日，克伦威尔解散了残余 议会。 他把他们赶出议会时说： “你们已经占位太久了，你们最近 做了太多好事……离开吧，我说， 从此我们和你们一刀两断。看在上 帝的份上，去吧！” 1653—1659 年间，克伦威尔作为 “护国公”直接统治着英格兰。 这一时期在英国历史上被称为“联 邦”时期。 但克伦威尔于1659年9月3日去世 了。 然后议会又重新掌权。

paragraphs.en: But after 10 years the people of England were sick of strange new governments. They wanted to be ruled by a KingAND Parliament as in the old days. At this point, Charles' eldest son, Charles II was living in France. Charles II saw an opportunity. Charles II was living in France because his mother was French and the French King, Louis XIV was his cousin. So the French court protected him. Here is his family tree. Now Charles II made an offer to the English Parliament. 1) He would forgive everyone who fought against him. 2) Parliament would have full control of taxation.
paragraphs.zh: 但 10 年后，英国人民厌倦了奇怪的 新政府。他们希望像过去那样，由 国王和议会来统治。 此时，住在法国的查理的长子—— 查理二世看到了机会。 查理二世住在法国，是因为他的母 亲是法国人，而法国国王路易十四 是他的表弟。所以法国法院保护了 他。 这是他的家谱。 查理二世向英格兰议会提出了一个 建议。 1）他会原谅所有曾经与他作对的 人。 2）议会将完全控制税收。 3）议会将控制军队。

paragraphs.en: 3) Parliament would control the army. 4) He promised religious toleration for all Protestant churches. Parliament accepted this offer and invited Charles II to come home and be King again. Charles travelled to England on May 25th, 1660. And on May 29th he was welcomed into London by cheering crowds. And he was crowned King that same day. The English Civil War was over, but the principal of Parliamentary co-rule had been permanently established. Charles II ruled England from 1660
paragraphs.zh: 4）他承诺对所有新教教会实行宗教 宽容。 议会接受了这个提议，邀请查理二 世回国再次成为国王。 1660 年 5 月 25 日，查尔斯回到英 格兰。 5 月 29 日，欢呼的人群欢迎他来到 伦敦。 就在当天，他加冕为王。 英国内战结束了，但议会联合统治 的原则已经永久确立。 查理二世在 1660—1685 年间统治

paragraphs.en: until 1685 when he died of a stroke. But his wife Catherine of Braganza was unable to have children so Charles' heir was his younger brother James II. James II was married to an English woman named Anne Hyde and they had two daughters: Princess Mary and PrincessAnne. Like the majority of England, James II and his family were all Protestants. But thenAnne Hyde died on March 31st, 1671. And then James II announced that he was a Catholic in 1673/ This of course alarmed the Protestant Parliament, because it meant that the heir apparent was a Catholic..
paragraphs.zh: 着英国，与 1685 年死于中风。 但他的妻子，来自布拉干扎王朝的 凯瑟琳无法生育，所以查理的继承 人是他的弟弟詹姆斯二世。 詹姆斯二世娶了一个叫安妮·海德 的英国女人，他们有两个女儿：玛 丽公主和安妮公主。 和大多数英格兰人一样，詹姆斯二 世和他的家人都是新教徒。 但安妮·海德于 1671 年 3 月 31 日 去世。 1673 年，詹姆斯二世宣布他是天主 教徒。 这当然引起了新教议会的警觉，因 为这意味着法定继承人是天主教 徒。

paragraphs.en: And then, on November 23rd, 1673, James married an Italian Catholic Princess named Mary of Modena. This alarmed the English people even more! Because James II was the heir to the throne! If he had any children with Mary, then they would be Catholics.And if they had a son, then he would be first in line to the throne. So from 1679 to 1681 Parliament had a big fight with Charles trying to make it illegal for James to be the heir. This fight is called the Exclusion Crisis by English historians.
paragraphs.zh: 1673 年 11 月 23 日，詹姆斯迎娶了 意大利天主教公主——摩德纳的玛 丽。 这更加惊动了英国人民！因为詹姆 斯二世是王位的继承人！如果他和 玛丽有孩子，那么孩子就是天主教 徒。如果他们生了个儿子，那么这 个男孩将是王位的第一继承人。 1679—1681 年间，议会为了让詹姆 斯成为非法继承人而和查理大吵了 一架。这场斗争被英国历史学家称 为废黜危机。

paragraphs.en: Hello and welcome back to our lecture series on world history. Today we will learn about Louis XIV: the Sun King. Our lecture will focus on 1610 to 1715; here is what that looks like on our timeline. In the 1600s, the monarchs of England engaged in a power struggle with Parliament. The English Civil War and the Glorious Revolution were two major events in that struggle. By the end, parliament had won, emerging as the supreme branch of government. But in France, things went the other way. There, the King’s power
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到世界历史系列课 程。今天我们学习的内容是路易十 四：太阳王。 这节课我们将目光转向 1610 到 1715 年的这段时间；这是我们的时 间轴。 17 世纪，英国的君主们与议会陷入 了一场权力斗争。 英国内战和光荣革命是这场斗争中 的两大主要事件。 到最后，议会获得胜利，成为政府 的最高权力部门。 但在法国，情况却完全相反。国王 的权力在这里越来越大。

paragraphs.en: increased. This timeline shows English and French monarchs from 1600 until 1714. In France, the timeline starts with Henry IV, who won the Wars of Religion. We learned about him in previous classes. Henry IV was assassinated in 1610. His son Louis XIII then became King. Louis XIII was only 9 when his father died, so his mother Marie de Medici ruled France from 1610 until 1617. In 1617 Louis was old enough to assume the throne. Louis XIII marriedAnne ofAustria (a Hapsburg princess) and the
paragraphs.zh: 这条时间线列出了 1600 年到 1714 年间英国和法国的君主。 法国的时间线是从赢得了宗教战争 的亨利四世开始的。我们在以前的 课上了解过他。 亨利四世于 1610 年被暗杀。 他的儿子路易十三继位。 路易十三在他的父亲去世时只有 9 岁 ， 所 以 法 国 由 他 的 母 亲 玛 丽·德·美第奇在 1610 年到 1617 年之间代为统治。 1617 年，路易已经到了可以继承王 位的年龄。 路易十三娶了西班牙菲利普三世的 女儿，奥地利的安妮(哈布斯堡的公

paragraphs.en: daughter of Phillip III of Spain. Later in life, when France and Spain fought,Anne would support her husband's country over that of her Spanish father. The early marriage years forAnne and Louis was difficult.Anne had trouble bearing children. She had many miscarriages and failed pregnancies. Louis began to rule directly in 1617 as well. The next year the Defenestration of Prague began the 30-Years war. In 1624 Louis XIII's chief minister became a man named Cardinal Richelieu. Together Louis and Richelieu
paragraphs.zh: 主)为妻。 在后来法国和西班牙开战时，安妮 选择支持她的丈夫，而不是她的西 班牙父亲。 安妮和路易斯早年的婚姻生活很艰 难。安妮有生育上的问题，曾经多 次流产。 路易从 1617 年开始接过统治的大 权。 第二年，“布拉格抛窗事件”引发 了 30 年战争。 1624 年，红衣主教黎塞留成为了路 易十三的首席大臣。 1628 年，路易和黎塞留一同镇压了

paragraphs.en: crushed a Huguenot rebellion at the French city of La Rochelle in 1628. This picture shows Richelieu overseeing the siege of La Rochelle. Louis and Richelieu then intervened in the 30-Years War first by supporting Sweden from 1630 to 1635. After GustavusAdolphus died, they realized that France needed to fight the Hapsburgs directly. This led to the bloodiest phase of the 30-Years War from 1635 to 1648. During this phase, French won a major victory against the Spanish Hapsburgs at the Battle of Rocroi in 1643. Until then, the Spanish army was considered the best in Europe.
paragraphs.zh: 法国拉罗谢尔市的胡格诺派叛乱。 图中的景象正是黎塞留指挥围攻拉 罗谢尔的场面。 1630 年到 1635 年间，路易和黎塞 留通过支持瑞典，首先介入了 30 年战争。 古斯塔夫斯·阿道夫斯死后，他们 意识到法国需要直接与哈布斯堡王 朝作战。 1635 年到 1648 年的这段时期，正 是 30 年战争中最血腥的阶段。 在此期间，法国人在 1643 年的罗克 鲁瓦战役中面对西班牙哈布斯堡王 朝大获全胜。在此之前，西班牙军 队是公认的欧洲最强军队。

paragraphs.en: Rocroi is a major marker in the decline of Spanish power and rise of French power that occurred during the 1600s. Richelieu did not outlast the 30-Years War. He died in 1642. Cardinal Mazerin succeeded him as First Minister of State. During this time, a surprise occurred. In 1638Anne gave birth to a healthy baby boy when she was 37 years old. This was Louis XIV. The next year in 1640, she gave birth to another son, Philippe I, the day before her 39th birthday. Anne treasured her two sons. Three years later, Louis XIII died and was succeeded by his 5-year-old son Louis XIV.
paragraphs.zh: 罗克鲁瓦战役是 17 世纪期间西班 牙势力衰落和法国势力崛起的重要 转折点。 黎塞留并没有熬过 30 年战争。他死 于 1642 年。 红衣主教马泽林接替他成为首席大 臣。 在这期间，发生了一件意想不到的 事。1638 年，安妮于 37 岁的高龄 生下了一个健康的男婴。他就是路 易十四。 第二年，也就是 1640 年，在她 39 岁生日的前一天，她生下了另一个 儿子，菲利普一世。 安妮非常珍爱她的两个儿子。 三年后，路易十三去世，他五岁的 儿子路易十四继位。

paragraphs.en: At the center of Versailles was the King himself. The entire nobility of France became obsessed with attracting his favor and attention. Louis had achieved his objective. He’d brought the nobility under his control. Now the center of everything, Louis called himself: "The Sun King." And that was true more broadly. Under Louis XIV, France became the most powerful country in Europe. International politics revolved around it, like planets around the Sun. Louis decided that he loved war. It was the best way to increase the power and glory of France. Louis fought many wars during his
paragraphs.zh: 凡尔赛宫的中心是国王本人。整个 法国的贵族阶层都把心思放在吸引 他的青睐和关注上。 路易达到了他的目的。他控制了贵 族阶层。 现在，路易成为了万物的中心，他 自称为:“太阳王。” 从某种意义来说，这是正确的。 在路易十四的统治下，法国成为欧 洲最强大的国家。国际政治围绕着 它运转，就像行星围绕着太阳一样。 路易热爱战争。这是增强法国实力 和荣耀的最佳途径。 在他漫长的统治期间，路易参加了

paragraphs.en: long reign. Two were small. The first was the War of Devolution. Early in his reign, Louis claimed that several towns on the French border belonged to him, not to the Spanish Netherlands. From 1667 to 1668, Louis fought a short war to capture those towns. Louis added Saint-Omer, Lile and Cambrai to France, which remain French towns today. Another small war was the War of the Reunions from 1683 to 1684. This was another war for the control of towns on the border of France. This victory gave France the cities of Luxembourg and Strasbourg. Luxembourg is an independent
paragraphs.zh: 许多战争。其中有两场是较小规模 的。 首先是遗产战争。 在他统治的早期，路易声称法国边 境上的几个城镇属于他，而不是西 班牙的。 从 1667 年到 1668 年，路易为夺取 这些城镇进行了一场短暂的战争。 路易将圣奥默、利勒和坎布赖划入 了法国的版图，这些城市至今仍是 法国的城镇。 另一场小规模的战争是 1683 年到 1684 年的统一战争。 这是另一场争夺法国边境城镇控制 权的战争。 这次胜利使法国获得了卢森堡和斯 特拉斯堡。 卢森堡今天是一个独立的国家。但

paragraphs.en: country today. But Strasbourg is a French city. Louis also fought several large wars, and these were much more destructive. The first was the Franco Dutch war of 1672 to 1678. It did great damage to the Dutch and set the course for the rest of Louis' reign. It began in 1670 when Louis XIV signed a secret treaty with his cousin Charles II of England. Under this “Treaty of Dover” They agreed to attack the Dutch together. This meant that after Charles died the next in line would either be one of theAustrian Hapsburgs, who were cousins of his, or one of Louis XIV's grandsons.
paragraphs.zh: 是斯特拉斯堡是一个法国城市。 路易也打过几次大规模的战争，这 些战争更具破坏性。第一次是 1672 年至 1678 年的法荷战争。 它对荷兰人造成了毁灭性的打击， 并为路易的统治奠定了基础。 1670 年，路易十四与他的堂兄英国 查理二世签署了一项秘密条约。 根据这个“多佛条约”，他们同意 共同攻击荷兰人。 这意味着查尔斯死后，他的下一个 继承人要么是他的堂兄弟，奥地利 的哈布斯堡家族成员；要么是路易 十四的孙子。

paragraphs.en: Either choice would upset the balance of power in Europe by making the French Bourbon family orAustrian Hapsburgs much more powerful. In the end Charles chose Louis' grandson Phillip as his heir. On November 1st, 1700, Charles died. His will reached Louis 8 days later, naming Philip his heir. Aweek later, Louis accepted, making his grandson the new King of Spain. This terrified the British, Dutch and Germans. That's because both Spain and France had large empires.
paragraphs.zh: 这两种选择都会使法国波旁家族或 奥地利哈布斯堡家族更加强大，从 而打破欧洲的权力平衡。 最后查尔斯选择路易的孙子菲利普 作为他的继承人。 1700 年 11 月 1 日，查理去世。 8 天后，他的遗嘱到达路易手中， 指定菲利普为他的继承人。 一周后，路易接受了，让他的孙子 成为西班牙的新国王。 这吓坏了英国人、荷兰人和德国人。 这是因为西班牙和法国都有庞大的 帝国殖民地。

paragraphs.en: By making Philip the King of Spain, Louis would basically unify the two Empires and become super-powerful. Agiant war began, with Europe divided into two sides. One side supported the Bourbons, the other supported the Hapsburgs. Today historians call this conflict "The War of the Spanish succession." Really it was a continuation of the 9-years war. Fighting resumed in the same places: the Rhine, Spain, the low countries and northern Italy. Fighting also continued inAmerica. This war saw great battles, like the battle of Blenheim. the battle of Ramilles.
paragraphs.zh: 菲利普成为西班牙国王后，路易可 以说基本上统一了两个帝国，变得 超级强大。 一场庞大的战争开始了，欧洲一分 为二。 一边支持波旁王朝，另一边支持哈 布斯堡王朝。 今天，历史学家称这场冲突为“西 班牙王位继承之战”。 实际上，这是九年战争的延续。战 斗在莱茵河、西班牙、低地国家和 意大利北部等地继续进行。 美国的战斗也在继续。 这场战争中，发生了许多伟大的战 役，比如布伦海姆战役。 拉梅斯战役。

paragraphs.en: The battle ofAlmansa And the battle of Malplaquet.
paragraphs.zh: 阿尔曼萨之战 还有马尔普拉盖战役。

paragraphs.en: Hello and welcome back to our lecture series on World History. Today we will learn about theAge of Enlightenment. This lesson will focus on the years between 1660 and 1789. Here is what that looks like on our timeline. Last time we discussed the war of the Spanish Succession: the last of the Louis XIV's great wars. Here is a map of Europe at the beginning of that war in 1700. And here it is in at the end in 1714. These were the results of the war when finally ended in 1714: Louis got to keep his grandson Phillip the throne but Philip had to give up all Spain's non-Spanish European territory.
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到世界历史系列课 程。今天我们学习的是启蒙运动。 这节课的内容主要发生于 1660 年 到 1789 年间。这是我们的时间轴。 上节课我们讲到了西班牙王位继 承战争：路易十四的最后一场大 战。 这是 1700 年战争刚开始时的欧洲 地图。 这是 1714 年末的情况。 1714 年战争最终结束时的结果是 这样的： on 路易得以让他的孙子菲利浦继承 of 王位，但菲利浦不得不放弃所有西 班牙以外的欧洲领土。

paragraphs.en: This included the Spanish Netherlands, Southern Italy, Corsica and Sardinia and Sicily. Then Louis died two years later on September 1st, 1715. His grandson Philip had become the King of Spain while his great Grandson Louis XV was the new King of France. His grand nephew Philippe II would be the regent for Louis while he was still a boy. This regency period lasted from 1715 until 1723 as Louis XV was growing up. In 1717, Philip V decided that he wanted to retake Spain's Italian territory. He also refused to renounce all claims to the French throne. This led to a brief war in Italy with Bourbon Spain on one side and Britain,
paragraphs.zh: 这包括西属的荷兰、意大利南部、 科西嘉岛、撒丁岛和西西里岛地 区。 两年后的 1715 年 9 月 1 日，路易 去世。 他的孙子菲利普成为西班牙的国 王，而他的曾孙路易十五成了法国 的新国王。 他的侄孙菲利普二世成为路易的 摄政王，因为路易在那时还是个孩 子。 这一摄政时期从 1715 年开始，随 着路易十五的长大到1723年结束。 1717 年，腓力五世决定夺回西班牙 的意大利领土。他还拒绝声明放弃 对法国王位的要求。 这导致在意大利发生了一场短暂 的战争，一方是西班牙波旁王朝，

paragraphs.en: France, the Holy Roman Empire and the Dutch on the other. This war is called the war of the QuadrupleAlliance and it ended in 1720 without changing very much. The next big conflict came from 1733 to 1735 this one was called the War of the Polish succession and it was fought over who should be the next King of Poland. Then in 1740, the Holy Roman Emperor, Charles VI died from eating poisonous mushrooms. Charles had no sons, only daughters. Here is a picture of Charles with his family. Here is Charles' family tree. When died his crown passed to his daughter Maria-Theresa. When Emperor Charles VI died, Charles
paragraphs.zh: 另一方是英国、法国、神圣罗马帝 国和荷兰。 这场战争 1720 年结束，被称为四 重联盟战争。它并没有对局势产生 太大变化。 第二次大的冲突发生在 1733 年到 1735 年，这场战争围绕着下一任波 兰国王的人选而展开，被称为“波 兰王位继承战争”。 1740 年，神圣罗马帝国皇帝查理六 世因为食用了毒蘑菇而死去。 查理没有儿子，只有女儿。这是查 尔斯和他家人的图片。 这是查理的家谱。当他死后，他的 王冠传给了他的女儿玛丽亚-特蕾 莎。 当查理六世皇帝去世时，巴伐利亚

paragraphs.en: Albert, the Prince-Elector of Bavaria, saw an opportunity to become the first non-Hapsburg Holy Roman Emperor in over 300 years. The King of Prussia, Frederick II, also saw an opportunity to seize some northern Hapsburg territory. This led to another big war called The War of theAustrian Succession which lasted from 1740 to 1748. France saw an opportunity to weaken the Hapsburgs, and Spain hoped to retake some of its territory in Italy. Meanwhile Britain decided to support the Hapsburg's against their Spanish and French rivals. So fighting broke out all across Europe. Like the 9-years war and the War of the Spanish Succession, the War of the
paragraphs.zh: 的选帝侯储，查尔斯·阿尔伯特从 中看到了机会，他想成为 300 多年 来首位非哈布斯堡出身的神圣罗 马帝国皇帝。 普鲁士国王腓特烈二世想趁机夺 取哈布斯堡王朝北部的部分领土。 这导致了另一场大战，即 1740 年 至1748年的奥地利王位继承战争。 法国希望借此削弱哈布斯堡王朝， 而西班牙则想要夺回其在意大利 的部分领土。 与此同时，英国决定支持哈布斯堡 王朝，对抗西班牙和法国。 于是战争在整个欧洲爆发了。 就像9年战争和西班牙王位继承战 争一样，奥地利王位继承战争也是

paragraphs.en: Austrian Succession was a world war. It saw conflict in theAmericas, the Mediterranean, Scotland, the Low Countries, Germany, Italy, and India. When the war finally ended in 1748, things were largely unchanged. Here is a map of Europe before the war. And here it is after the war.As you can see, there weren't too many changes to European borders from this war. The only major change was that Prussia was able to take Silesia away from Austria. This doubled the size of Prussia and marked its emergence as one of Europe's great powers. As you might remember, Prussia began as the Order of the Teutonic Knights. In 1525 the Grandmaster of the Teutonic Knights was a man namedAlbert of
paragraphs.zh: 一场世界大战。 美洲、地中海、苏格兰、低地国家、 德国、意大利和印度都参与了这场 战争。 当战争最终于 1748 年结束时，局 势基本没有改变。 这是战前的欧洲地图。 这是战后的情况。正如你所看到 的，这场战争并没有给欧洲各国的 版图带来太多的变化。 唯一的重大变化是普鲁士把西里 西亚从奥地利夺了过来。这使普鲁 士的面积扩大了一倍，标志着它成 为欧洲强国之一。 你们可能还记得，普鲁士最初是由 条顿骑士团领导的。 1525 年，条顿骑士团的宗师是普鲁 士的阿尔伯特。由于宗师不允许结

paragraphs.en: Prussia. He was unhappy because the Grandmaster was not allowed to marry. So in 1525,Albert converted to Lutheranism in order to marry and produce an heir. Albert of Brandenburg was followed by his sonAlbert Frederick who ruled Prussia until the beginning of the 30 Years War. The crown then passed to Albert-Frederick's son-in-law John Sigismund. From there it went to John's son George William. From there it went to Frederick William who saw Prussia through the end of the 30 Years’War and second half of the 17th century. Next came Frederick I who lead Prussia through the Nine Years’ War and the War of the Spanish Succession.
paragraphs.zh: 婚，他对此很不满意。 1525 年，阿尔伯特皈依路德教以便 结婚并生育继承人。 阿尔伯特的位子由他的儿子阿尔 伯特·弗雷德里克继承，后者在三 十年战争开始前一直统治着普鲁 士。 然后王位传给了阿尔伯特·弗雷德 里克的女婿约翰·西吉斯蒙德。接 下来约翰的儿子乔治·威廉继承了 王位。 随后王位传给了弗雷德里克·威 廉，他于 17 世纪下半叶领导普鲁 士，并见证了 30 年战争的结束。 接下来是腓特烈一世，他领导普鲁 士经历了九年战争和西班牙王位 继承战争。

paragraphs.en: During this time Frederick gained the right to call himself King and not just Duke. Frederick I was followed by his son Frederick William I who built up the army, country and treasury. When Frederick William I died in 1740 he left Prussia in excellent shape for his son Frederick II, also known as Frederick the Great. Prussia was a small state without much the way of natural resources. So it made itself great by focusing on its army. Both Frederick William I and Frederick the Great trained their army and made service in the armed forces the key to political advancement. Discipline was strict and the Prussians practiced conscription.
paragraphs.zh: 在这段时间里，腓特烈获得了国王 的称号，而不仅仅是公爵。 腓特烈一世的继位者，他的儿子腓 特烈·威廉一世建立了军队、国家 和国库。 1740 年，腓特烈·威廉一世去世， 他将一个强大的普鲁士传给了他 的儿子腓特烈二世，即腓特烈大 帝。 in 普鲁士是一个小国，自然资源匮 乏。 因此普鲁士将重心转向军队，从而 变得强大。腓特烈威廉一世和腓特 烈大帝都操练军队，并把在军队中 服役作为加官进爵的必要条件。 普鲁士实行征兵制，纪律严明。

paragraphs.en: All of this meant that the Prussians had the best troops in Europe by 1740 and with 80,000 soldiers, the also had the 4th largest army. Frederick the Great used this army to make Prussia formidable in the War of theAustrian Succession and seize Silesia. But there was tension between the great powers and in 1756, Europe broke out into another Great War. This war is called the Seven Years’War. This war involved an alliance of Great Britain and Prussia against France, Spain, the Holy Roman Empire,Austria, Russia and Sweden. In this war the British did something different from all their other wars with France. The Prime Minister William Pitt realized that it was much more valuable for Britain to focus on attacking France's
paragraphs.zh: 这意味着到了 1740 年，普鲁士在 拥有欧洲最精锐的 8 万名士兵，同 时这也是欧洲的第四大军队。 这支军队使腓特烈大帝带领普鲁 士在奥地利王位继承战争中所向 披靡，并夺取了西里西亚。 但是大国之间的关系持续紧张， 1756 年，欧洲爆发了另一场大战。 这场战争被称为“七年战争”。 这场战争中，英国和普鲁士联合起 来，对抗法国、西班牙、神圣罗马 帝国、奥地利、俄罗斯和瑞典。 在这场战争中，英国人采取了一些 以前与法国交战时没有使用过的 手段。首相威廉·皮特意识到，对 英国来说，更有价值的是集中精力 攻击法国的海外殖民地，而不是专

paragraphs.en: overseas empire rather than focus on Europe. Meanwhile France decided to focus on Prussia and did not send help to her colonies. As a result, the British were able to capture France's Canadian colonies in 1759 and Indian colonies in 1761. Meanwhile the poor Prussians had to fight off theAustrians, French, other German states and Russians. But with the support of English money and the discipline of their strong army, they survived. By the end of the war, nothing had changed in the map of Europe. The Prussians had managed to hold onto their conquered territory from the War of the Austrian Succession.
paragraphs.zh: 注于欧洲战场。 与此同时，法国决定把战略重点放 在普鲁士，因而没有向殖民地提供 援助。 结果，英国人在 1759 年占领了法 国的加拿大殖民地，在 1761 年占 领了印度殖民地。 与此同时，可怜的普鲁士人不得不 与奥地利、法国、德国各邦以及俄 国作战。 但依靠着英国金钱的支持和他们 强大军队的纪律，他们活了下来。 战争结束时，欧洲的版图没有任何 变化。普鲁士人设法保住了他们在 奥地利王位继承战争中征服的领 土。

paragraphs.en: But the English had won a huge victory in India. Their conquest of the subcontinent began with this war. And in NorthAmerica their gains were even bigger. Here is a map of North America before the Seven Years’War. And here it is afterwards. The Seven Years War marks the moment when the British became the undisputed masters NorthAmerica. And more than anything else, the Seven Years War set the stage for theAmerican Revolution, just 12 years later. But for now, let us end our discussion of politics and war here in 1763. Because while all this fighting was going, an even more important change was occurring in Europe in the world of ideas. This period is known as the Enlightenment. The Enlightenment is sometimes called
paragraphs.zh: 但是英国人在印度赢得了巨大的 胜利。他们对次大陆的征服始于这 场战争。 而在北美，他们的收益甚至更大。 这是北美七年前的地图。 这是战争结束以后的情况。七年战 争标志着英国成为北美无可争议 of 的统治者。 最重要的是，七年战争为 12 年后 的美国独立战争奠定了基础。 讲到这里，让我们在 1763 年这个 时间点把政治和战争暂时告一段 落。因为当战争进行得如火如荼之 时，一个更重要的变化正在欧洲的 思想界发生。这一时期被称为启蒙 运动时期。 启蒙运动有时又被称为“理性时

paragraphs.en: the "Age of Reason" and it grew out of the Scientific Revolution just like the Scientific Revolution grew out of the Renaissance. At the heart of the enlightenment lay the belief that humans should be free to think, speak and reason publicly, and that reason should determine our understanding of the world and the rules of human society. During the 17th and 18th century other people began to spread scientific ideas the general public. Bernard de Fontenelle explained the Copernican world-view in a book Conversations on the Plurality of Worlds (1686) which are presented as a conversation between an aristocratic woman and her lover under starry sky. The Englishman JosephAddison created a newspaper called The Spectator.
paragraphs.zh: 代”，它诞生于科学革命，正如科 学革命诞生于文艺复兴。 启蒙运动的中心思想在于，人类应 有公开思考、发言和辩论的自由， 我们对世界和人类社会运行规律 的认识应由理性决定。 17 至 18 世纪期间，有些人开始向 公众传播科学思想。伯纳德·德·丰 特奈尔在《关于多重世界的对话》 一书中，通过一个贵族妇女和她的 爱人在星空下的对话，阐释了哥白 尼体系的世界观。 英国人约瑟夫·阿迪森创办了一份 名为《旁观者》的报纸。

paragraphs.en: Addison hoped that this paper would: "bring philosophy out of closets and libraries, schools and colleges, to dwell clubs, and assemblies, at tea-tables and coffee houses." That reference to coffee and tea is important, because the late 1600s is the time when Western Europeans began drinking tea and coffee for the first time. Coffee first reached England in 1637 and Tea in 1615. But both took a few decades to catch on. And of course, coffee and tea were special drinks because they both contain caffeine! Before this period the main drink was beer. In this time period people started drinking tea and coffee instead. Science Writer Steven Johnson has this
paragraphs.zh: 阿迪森希望这份报纸能够： “让哲学走出书柜、图书馆、学校 和学院，走进俱乐部、集会、茶桌 in 和咖啡馆。” 之所以提到咖啡和茶，是因为西欧 人正是在 17 世纪晚期开始饮用茶 和咖啡的。 咖啡和茶分别于 1637 年和 1615 年 传入英国。但两者都花了几十年时 间才流行起来。 当然，咖啡和茶都是非常特别的饮 料，因为它们都含有咖啡因! 在此之前，人们常喝的饮料是啤 酒。到了这段时期，人们开始喝茶 和咖啡。 to 科学作家史蒂文·约翰逊说:“理

paragraphs.en: say: "It’s not an accident that the age of reason accompanies the rise of caffeinated beverages! The culture went from drinking alcohol, a depressant, to tea and coffee, which are stimulants!" This process was helped by the arrival coffee shops which sold tea and coffee. The first cafe in English history opened in 1650 and they became more and more widespread over the following decades. At coffee shops people from all over society would meet to drink and talk and share ideas.And of course everybody was jazzed up by all that caffeine! Thinkers began applying the rationalism of science to thinking about society and its laws. In his books: Two Treatises of Government andALetter Concerning Toleration, John Locke argues that political power comes from the consent of the governed and that human beings
paragraphs.zh: 性的时代与咖啡因饮料的兴起出 现在同一时代，这绝非偶然！人们 从喝酒到喝茶和咖啡，这简直就是 从镇静剂和兴奋剂的区别！” 出售茶叶和咖啡的商店的出现，更 是促进了这一进程。英国历史上第 一家咖啡馆于 1650 年开业，在接 下来的几十年里，咖啡馆变得越来 越普遍。 在咖啡馆里，来自社会各界的人们 聚在一起喝茶喝咖啡、聊天、分享 想法。当然，人们都因为咖啡因而 兴奋起来！ 思想家们开始将科学的理性主义 应用在对社会与法律的思考之上。 约翰·洛克在他的著作《政府论》 和《论宗教宽容》中指出，政治权 力来自于被统治者，因而人类拥有 人权。

paragraphs.en: have human rights. For these ideas John Locke is viewed as the "Father of Liberalism". Another thinker was the French Huguenot Pierre Bayle. Bayle had to flee France for the Netherlands during the Dragonnades of Louis XIV. His magazine News from the Republic Letters and his book Historical and Critical Dictionary argued that all human beliefs must be tested by reason and common sense: Bayle wrote: "Any particular dogma, whatever it may be, whether it is advanced on the authority of the Scriptures, or whatever else may be its origins, is to be regarded as false if it clashes with the clear and definite conclusions of the natural
paragraphs.zh: 约翰·洛克因此被视为“自由主义 之父”。 另一位思想家是法国的胡格诺派 教徒皮埃尔·贝尔。在路易十四统 治时期，贝尔不得不从法国逃往荷 兰。 of 他的《文学界新闻》杂志和《历史 与批判词典》主张，人类所有的信 仰都必须通过理性和常识来检验： 贝尔写道： “任何特定的教条，无论是根据圣 经的权威提出的，又或是出自任何 人之口，如果它与自然理解的清晰 而明确的结论相冲突，那就是错误 的。”

paragraphs.en: understanding." This spirit of skeptical thinking was further advanced by the Scottish philosopher David Hume. In his workAn Essay Concerning Human Understanding he argued that nothing, not even God or our own existence could be known for sure. Like other Enlightenment thinkers David Hume's skepticism bordered on atheism. He wrote: "The Christian Religion not only was at first attended by miracles, but even now cannot be believed by any reasonable person without one." During those 8 years, the French government was less powerful and censorship weakened. That's why many historians think that the Enlightenment began in France with the
paragraphs.zh: 这种怀疑主义的精神由苏格兰哲 学家大卫·休谟进一步发扬光大。 在他的著作《人类理解论》中，他 认为没有任何东西，甚至包括上帝 或我们自身的存在，是可以确定 的。 和其他启蒙思想家一样，大卫·休 谟的怀疑论近乎无神论。他写道: “基督教从一开始就是伴随着神 迹出现的，除非能够亲眼见证一个 神迹的发生，否则任何一个理性的 人都不能相信它。” 在这 8 年里，法国政府的强权有所 减弱，审查制度也有所放松。 因此，许多历史学家认为法国的启 蒙运动始于路易十四之死。

paragraphs.en: death of Louis XIV. So books critical of France began to appear. The writer Baron de Montesquieu wrote a book called Persian Letters which takes the point of view of an imaginary Persian traveler in France. From this outsider perspective, Montesquieu could criticize everything he found ridiculous in French society. Montesquieu developed the ideas of John Locke even further in his book: The Spirit of the Laws he argued that political systems should be different in different societies according to local customs, beliefs, economy and climate. Montesquieu also proposed a system of checks and balances and the separation powers. This way it would be difficult any one person in government to gain too much power.
paragraphs.zh: 于是，当时出现了一些批判法国的 书籍。作家巴伦·德·孟德斯鸠出 版了《波斯人信札》，书中虚构了 一个居住在法国的波斯旅人，并讲 述了他的所见所闻。从这个局外人 的角度出发，孟德斯鸠对他所见的 法国社会的荒谬之处进行了批评。 孟德斯鸠在他的著作《法律的精 神》中进一步发展了约翰·洛克的 思想，他认为不同社会的政治制度 应该根据当地的习俗、信仰、经济 和气候而有所改变。 孟德斯鸠还提出了一套制衡和分 of 权的制度。这使得任何一个政府官 for 员都难以独揽大权。

paragraphs.en: This idea later became the basis for the US Constitution and theAmerican system of government. His contemporary, the writer François-MarieArouet wrote under the pen name "Voltaire". Voltaire also criticized French society in his book, Letters Concerning the English Nation, which praised the English for their freedom of speech, and criticized France for its lack of free speech protection. Voltaire had fled France when his work got him in trouble. But in 1733 he returned to France and met Émilie du Châtelet a brilliant and wealthy French woman who became Voltaire's lover and supporter. In 1733 Emile wanted to join a group of intellectuals in a coffee house, but was refused entry because of her sex. So she dressed up in male clothes and went
paragraphs.zh: 这一思想后来成为美国宪法和美 国政体的基础。 与他同时代的作家弗朗索瓦-马 利·阿鲁埃以笔名“伏尔泰”写作。 伏尔泰在他的著作《英国通信》 （注：又叫《哲学通信》）中也批 判了法国社会，赞扬了英国人的言 论自由，并批评法国缺乏对言论自 由的保护。 伏尔泰的作品给他招来了麻烦，于 是他逃离了法国。但在 1733 年， 他回到法国并遇到了一位聪明富 有的法国女人，艾米丽·杜夏特莱， 她后来成为了伏尔泰的爱人和支 持者。 1733 年，艾米丽想加入一个咖啡馆 里的知识分子团体，却因为她的性 别而被拒之门外。但她穿上男性的 衣服，还是去了。

paragraphs.en: anyway. Emile was also fascinated by science and did many experiments in her country house. Later on, Voltaire travelled to Prussia and was the private tutor of Frederick the Great, a King who admired Enlightenment thought, even though he maintained absolute control of his kingdom. Frederick the Great and Catherine the Great of Russia are examples of what historians call EnlightenedAbsolutism. This term is a bit of a contradiction. But it was a real phenomenon. These monarchs admired the Enlightenment and sponsored some of thinkers. But they completely rejected ideals of freedom and democracy that came out of the enlightenment.
paragraphs.zh: 艾米丽对科学也很着迷，并在她乡 下的房子里做了很多实验。 后来，伏尔泰去了普鲁士，成为腓 特烈大帝的私人教师。尽管腓特烈 大帝对自己的王国拥有绝对的控 制权，但他是一位欣赏启蒙思想的 国王。 腓特烈大帝和俄国叶卡捷琳娜大 帝就是历史学家所说的“开明专制 主义”的范例。 这个名词似乎有点自相矛盾。但却 又是一个真实存在的现象。 这些君主崇拜启蒙运动，并资助了 一些思想家。但是他们却将来自启 the 蒙运动的自由和民主的理想完全 拒之门外。

paragraphs.en: Voltaire continued to defend the ideas of skeptical inquiry and tolerance. He published two more books towards the end of his life: The Philosophical Dictionary and the Treatise on Tolerance. In the Treatise on Tolerance Voltaire argues that all people should tolerate each other, writing: Voltaire wrote: "Christians ought to tolerate one another. I will go even further and say that we ought to look upon all men as our brothers. What! Call a Turk, a Jew, a Siamese my brother? Yes of course, for are we not all children of the same father and the creatures of the same God?" Voltaire was deeply skeptical of religion, but in that quote you can see that he still uses ideas about humans being children of God to justify the idea of universal
paragraphs.zh: 伏尔泰继续坚持怀疑、探究和宽容 的思想。他晚年又出版了两本书: 《哲学辞典》和《论宽容》。 伏尔泰在《论宽容》中认为，所有 人都应该互相宽容: 伏尔泰写道: “基督徒应该互相宽容。不仅如 此，我们还应该把所有的人类都当 作兄弟看待。什么!把一个土耳其 人，犹太人，暹罗人叫做兄弟?你 没有听错，难道我们不都是上帝的 子民吗?” 伏尔泰对宗教深表怀疑，但在这段 话中，你可以看到他仍然用人类是 上帝之子的观点来论证宽容的思 想。

paragraphs.en: tolerance. I like this quote because it helps illustrate the essence of the enlightenment. I think it was a combination of Christian ideas involving the value of individuals and brotherhood of mankind, with a skeptical rationalism coming from the scientific tradition. Another important Enlightenment thinker was Jean-Jacques Rousseau who wrote Discourse on the Origins of Inequality (1755) argues that humans were free and equal before private property began creating inequality. His other famous book was The Social Contract which was published in 1762. In it he wrote: "Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains." The enlightenment also directed its attention to the economy.
paragraphs.zh: 我喜欢这句话，因为它有助于阐明 启蒙运动的本质。我认为那就是基 督教中关于个人价值和人类的手 足之情的思想，与科学惯有的怀疑 理性主义的结合。 另一位重要的启蒙思想家是让-雅 克•卢梭，他在《论不平等的起源》 (1755)一书中提出，在私有财产开 始造成不平等之前，人类是自由和 平等的。 他的另一本著作是 1762 年出版的 《社会契约论》。 他在书中写道:“人生而自由，但 却无处不受制约。” 启蒙运动还将注意力转向了经济。

paragraphs.en: Agroup of French thinkers called the Physiocrats were led by Francois Quesnay. They argued that economics its own set of laws based on supply-and-demand and the best way to manage to economy was for government to leave it alone. This doctrine of non-interference became known by the French term laissez-faire. This idea was taken even further by the Scottish thinkerAdam Smith who published The Wealth of Nations in 1776. In that book Smith supported the idea of laissez-faire by arguing that letting people pursue their own economic self-interest would bring the greatest benefit to society. Smith also discussed the benefits of the division of labor writing: "the greatest improvement in the
paragraphs.zh: 法国思想家弗朗索瓦·魁奈领导了 重农学派。他们认为，经济学有自 has 己的一套基于供求关系的规律，对 经济进行管理的最佳方式是让政 府对其放任自流。 这种不干涉主义被法国人称为“自 由放任主义”。 苏格兰思想家亚当•斯密在1776年 出版了《国富论》，进一步阐述了 这一观点。书中，史密斯支持自由 放任主义的观点，认为让人们追求 自己的经济利益将给社会带来最 大的利益。 斯密还讨论了分工的好处: “劳动生产率的极大提高……都

paragraphs.en: productive powers of labor... have been the effects of the division of labor." Another important thinker turned his attention to the law and crime. His name was Cesare Beccaria and he wrote the influential book: On Crimes and Punishment (1764) In that book, Becarria wrote that criminal law should try to deter and rehabilitate criminals rather than just punish wrongdoers. In his writing, Becarria argued strongly against capital punishment and torture. Two more thinkers, Denis Diderot and Jean Le Rond d'Alembert, wanted to organize all the world's knowledge into series of books. This was the world's first Encyclopedia. It took 21 years to finish, but when it was
paragraphs.zh: 是劳动分工的结果。” 另一位重要的思想家将注意力转 向法律和犯罪。 他就是切萨雷·贝卡里亚，他写了 一本很有影响力的书：《论犯罪与 刑罚》(1764) 贝卡里亚认为，刑法应该试图阻止 和改造罪犯，而不仅仅是惩罚犯错 者。 在他的书中，贝卡里亚强烈反对死 刑和酷刑。 另外两位思想家，丹尼斯·狄德罗 和让·勒朗·达朗贝尔，想把世界 上所有的知识汇集成一系列的书 籍。 这便是世界上第一部百科全书。它 足足花了 21 年，于 1772 年完成，

paragraphs.en: completed in 1772 it became perhaps the greatest of all Enlightenment works. The motto of the Encyclopedia was inscribed on the first pages, and it was good example of the Enlightenment spirit. "If there is something you know, communicate it. If there is something you don't know, search for it." Some female writers also began to argue for a greater role of women in society. MaryAstell wroteASerious Proposal to the Ladies (1694) arguing that women should have more educational opportunities. And Mary Wollstonecraft wroteA Vindication of the Rights of Woman which argued that all human beings
paragraphs.zh: 这可能是启蒙运动中最伟大的一 部作品。 百科全书的格言刻在了第一页，这 段话很好地代表了启蒙精神。 “你的知识和想法， 应多与人交流。 对于未知的事物， 应不断地追寻。” 一些女作家也开始主张女性在社 会中发挥更大的作用。 玛丽·阿斯特尔在《对女士们的严 肃建议》(1694)一书中，主张女性 应该有更多的受教育机会。 玛丽·沃斯通克拉夫特写了《女权 辩护》，主张每个人都应该享有平 等的权利。

paragraphs.en: should have equal rights. The late 1700s also saw a great increase in book publishing as you can see from this chart of published books in England. As all these books and ideas began to circulate around Europe in the 1700s, people wanted to meet together and discuss them. So people began hosting meetings known as salons where all of these new ideas could be discussed. The greatest salon organizer of this time was Madame Marie-Therese Geoffrin. Madame Geoffrin hosted many such meetings at her house and helped facilitate the human connections and discussions that made the Enlightenment possible. She also inspired many more men and women to host their own salons
paragraphs.zh: 我们可以从这张英国出版书籍的 图表中看出，18 世纪晚期，书籍出 版量出现了较高的增长。 随着 18 世纪这些书籍和思想在欧 洲传播，人们希望能够聚在一起讨 论它们。 于是，人们开始举办沙龙会议来交 流这些新想法。 这一时期最伟大的沙龙会议组织 者便是玛丽·泰瑞莎·杰奥弗林夫 人。 杰奥弗林夫人在她的家里主持了 许多这样的会议，帮助促进了人们 的联系和交流，从而使得启蒙运动 成为可能。她还鼓励更多的人举办 沙龙会议、成立讨论小组。

paragraphs.en: and discussion groups. This painting, titled An Evening at Madame Geoffrin's shows one of her salons in action. Madame Geoffrin is wearing the blue dress on the right side of the picture. All of these books and ideas and discussions transformed the way Western Europeans thought about the world in the 1700s.And eventually they would inspire two great revolutions which shattered the old Europe of Kings and Queens, and created the world we live in today. But that is a story for next time! I hope you enjoyed that lesson and want to learn more. If so I have two recommendations. The first is The Enlightenment:ABrief History with
paragraphs.zh: 这幅名为《杰奥弗林夫人家的一个 夜晚》的油画描绘了她家的沙龙会 议的场景。 画中右侧穿着蓝色连衣裙那位就 是杰奥弗林夫人。 所有这些书籍、思想和交流改变了 18 世纪西欧人对世界的看法，并最 终激发了两场伟大的革命，粉碎了 属于国王和女王们的旧欧洲，创造 了我们今天生活的世界。 但这是下节课的故事了! 我希望你喜欢这节课。如果你还余 兴未了，我可以给你们两个建议。 第一个是 《启蒙运动:文献简史》

paragraphs.en: Documents by Margaret C. Jacob The Second is the textbook The West in the World by Dennis Sherman & Joyce Salisbury Thank you very much!
paragraphs.zh: 作者是玛格丽特·C·雅各布。 第二个是一本教科书 《西方世界》 作者是丹尼斯·谢尔曼和乔伊斯·索 尔兹伯里 谢谢大家!

paragraphs.en: Last time we learned about how the British won the Seven Years War and were able to take over the French colonies in India and Canada. But even though the British won, the war left the Empire deeply in debt. Since the war had mainly benefited the American colonies, the British Parliament decided that it was fair to ask theAmerican Colonies to pay more in taxes. But some of the Colonists inAmerica were unhappy about this idea because the Colonists had no vote in Parliament. The Colonists argued that they should able to vote for any government that wanted to tax them. Their rallying cry was" no taxation
paragraphs.zh: 上节课我们学习了英国人是如何 赢得七年战争，成功取代了法国在 印度和加拿大的殖民者地位。 但是即便英国人获胜了，这场战争 也让帝国负债累累。 由于战争的主要受益者是北美殖 民地，于是为了公平起见，英国议 会决定要求北美殖民地上缴更多 的税收。 但是，北美的一些殖民者不开心 了，因为他们在议会中没有投票 权。 这些人声称他们理应有权在想要 征税的政府中拥有投票权。 他们的口号是是“无代表，无税

paragraphs.en: without representation." So for ten years, from 1764 until 1773, the British government in London and American colonists argued with each other over taxes. Parliament tried to create new taxes on sugar, paper, tea and other goods. TheAmericans countered with protests and boycotts. MostAmericans wanted to either be left alone, or to be given a voice in the English government, but a more radical group of men called the Sons of Liberty wanted something more. They wanted full independence for the colonies. So in order to Protest the tax on tea, the Sons of Liberty dressed up likeAmerican Indians and boarded a ship belonging to the East India Company.
paragraphs.zh: 收”。 所以在 1764—1773 这 10 年间，伦 the 敦的应该政府与北美殖民者就税 收问题不停地纠缠。 议会想要在糖、纸、茶及其他物品 上增设税目。 北美人则抗议抵制，进行反击。 大多数北美人的意愿是，要么不被 英国管，要么在英国政府中有一席 之地，但是一个叫做自由之子的激 进团体要求得更多。他们想要殖民 地完全独立。 所以，为了抵制茶叶税，自由之子 的成员伪装为美洲印第安人，登上 了东印度公司的船。

paragraphs.en: Once onboard they seized the cargo of and threw it into the harbor of Boston. all they destroyed 342 chests of tea. This event is called The Boston Tea Party and it happened on December 16th, 1773. This act enraged the government in London, who had been frustrated by 9 years ofAmerican resistance to what they thought were fair taxes. So in 1774 the Parliament responded with a series of laws that theAmericans called the IntolerableActs: 1) The port of Boston was closed to punish the entire city for the tea party. 2)Any British official accused of a crime would be tried in England notAmerica. 3) The self-government of Massachusetts was ended and all government officials would be chosen by the King instead of
paragraphs.zh: tea 一上船，他们就抢夺了货舱中的茶 In 叶，把它们扔下了波士顿港，他们 总共销毁了 342 箱茶叶。 这一事件史称“波士顿倾茶事件”， 发生于 1773 年 12 月 16 日。 伦敦政府听到这一消息后，勃然大 怒，他们对北美人在这 9 年里对他 们眼中的公平税收的抵制感到恼 火。 所以，在 1774 年，作为对此事件 的回应，议会颁布了一系列法律， 美国人称之为“不可容忍法案”： 1）关闭波士顿港，作为因倾茶事 件对整个城市的惩罚。 2）所有被指控犯罪的英国官员都 将在英国受审，而不是在北美。 3）结束马萨诸塞州的自治，所有 的政府官员都将由国王产生，而非 选举。

paragraphs.en: by elections. 4) The British had the right to seize buildings and use them for housing troops. This treatment of Massachusetts angered all of theAmerican colonies. Because what could be done to Massachusetts could be done to them. So from September 5th to October 26th, 1774, the colonists all sent representatives to Philadelphia to hold "Continental Congress" and discuss what to do. In the end the Congress decided to just petition the King for a change in policy, and to meet again if things did not get better. But next year things got worse. Massachusetts began organizing a militia and stockpiling weapons. The other
paragraphs.zh: 4）英国人有权占领建筑物并将其 用作军队的住房。 马萨诸塞州的遭遇激怒了所有美 国殖民地人民。因为马萨诸塞州的 遭遇也可能发生在自己州。 因此，从 1774 年 9 月 5 日到 10 月 26 日，殖民者都派代表到费城召开 “大陆会议”，讨论该怎么办。 最终，大会决定向国王请愿，要求 改变政策，如果情况没有好转，大 会将再次召集会议。 但第二年情况变得更糟了。马萨诸 塞州开始组织民兵并储备武器。其 他殖民地也开始这样做。

paragraphs.en: colonies started doing the same. OnApril 18th the British general in Boston heard that theAmericans were keeping some of these guns in the nearby town of Concord Massachusetts. When the British sent an army to confiscate those guns, fighting broke out between them and the militia of the surrounding towns. The first fighting began onApril 19th, 1775 at the towns of Concord and Lexington just outside Boston. This battle marked the beginning of the American Revolutionary War. In May of 1775 a Second Continental Congress met in Philadelphia to organize the resistance. They appointed George Washington as the leader of a New ContinentalArmy.
paragraphs.zh: 4月18日，波士顿的英国将军听说， 在附近的马萨诸塞州康科德镇，北 美人持有枪支。 于是英国人派遣军队去没收这些 枪支，就这样，他们和周围城镇的 民兵打了起来。 1775 年 4 月 19 日，第一场战斗在 波士顿郊外的康科德镇和列克星 敦镇打响了。 这场战斗标志着美国独立战争的 开始。 1775 年 5 月，第二届大陆会议在费 城召开，目的是组织抵抗运动。 会议确立了乔治·华盛顿为新大陆 军的领袖。

paragraphs.en: TheAmericans fought another battle with the British at Bunker Hill two months later on June 17th, 1775. The British drove off theAmerican force, but only after losing 1000 soldiers in the battle. And in March of 1776 Washington was able to drive the British out of Boston. The British made a temporary retreat to Halifax, Nova Scotia. And on July 4th, 1776 this Congress formally declared Independence from Britain. And on that day the United States of America was born. TheAmerican Declaration was filled with enlightenment ideas like these famous words: "We hold these truths to be self evident
paragraphs.zh: 2 个月后，1775 年 6 月 17 日，美 方与英方在邦克山又打了一仗。 英国人打败了美军，但也付出了失 去 1000 多名士兵的代价。 1776 年 3 月，华盛顿将英国人赶出 了波士顿。英军暂时撤退到新斯科 舍的哈利法克斯。 1776 年 7 月 4 日，代表大会正式宣 布脱离英国独立。 就在那一天，美利坚合众国诞生 了。 《独立宣言》充满了启蒙思想，比 如下面这句名言: “我们认为这些真理是不言而喻

paragraphs.en: that all men are created equal and are endowed by their creator with certain inalienable rights, among these are life, liberty and the pursuit of happiness." But the British returned with a much larger force that same month. New York fell on September 16th 1776. And the next year the British captured Philadelphia on September 11th, 1777. But theAmericans held on, and that same autumn they won a huge victory against the British at the Battle of Saratoga. After theAmerican victory at Saratoga, the French began to believe that the Americans could win. So they started sending theAmericans money and troops. And then the Spanish, and Dutch joined in, so after 1778 it became a war of 4
paragraphs.zh: 的：人人生而平等，造物主赋予他 们若干不可剥夺的权利，其中包括 生命权、自由权和追求幸福的权 利。 但同月，英军以更大的兵力卷土重 来。 1776 年 9 月 16 日，纽约沦陷。 1777 年 9 月 11 日，费城被英军拿 下。 但是美国人继续坚持战斗，同年秋 天，他们在萨拉托加战役中战胜了 英国人，取得了巨大的胜利。 美国人在萨拉托加获胜后，法国人 开始相信美国人能赢得最终的胜 利。 于是他们开始给予美国人资金和 兵力上的支持。 紧接着，西班牙、荷兰也站在美国 这边，就这样，1778 年，战争局面

paragraphs.en: against 1. Then after 3 more years of fighting, a combinedAmerican-French army defeated the British at the Battle of Yorktown and captured over 7000 British soldiers. The war was over for Britain. On September 3rd, 1783, the British recognized the independence of the United States by signing the Treaty of Paris. The British ambassadors were so unhappy about this that they refused to pose for the painting that commemorated the peace treaty. Canada remained part of the British Empire but the 13 colonies gained control of all the land between the East Coast and the Mississippi river.
paragraphs.zh: 变成了 4 对 1。 战争又持续了 3 年，美法联军在约 克镇之战中打败了英军，俘虏了 7000 多名英兵。 英国人彻底输了。 1783 年 9 月 3 日，英国签署了《巴 黎条约》，承认美国独立。 英国使者们当时非常不开心，他们 拒绝为纪念和平条约的画作摆造 型。 加拿大仍然是大英帝国的一部分， 但是 13 个殖民地控制了东海岸和 密西西比河之间的所有土地。

paragraphs.en: For Britain, theAmerican Revolution was a colossal defeat. But for the French was sweet revenge for their own defeat the Seven Years‘ War. But it also created a problem for the French. The spending of Louis XIV and Louis XV, the War of theAustrian Succession, the Seven Years War and the American Revolution had left them deeply in debt. From 1781 until 1789, the French King tried increasingly desperate ways of raising money to pay off his debt. He went through three different finance ministers: Turgot, Necker and Calonne. They tried all sorts of different financial reform schemes. But none of these worked. Eventually Louis XVI decided to do
paragraphs.zh: 美国独立战争对英国来说是一次 it 巨大的失败。但对在七年战争中失 in 利的法国人来说，这场胜利一雪前 耻。 但这也给法国带来了一个问题。路 易十四和路易十五的开支、奥地利 王位继承战争、七年战争和美国独 立战争使他们债台高筑。 从 1781 年到 1789 年，法国国王尝 试了越来越孤注一掷的筹资方式 来偿还债务。 他更换了三位财政部长：杜尔哥、 内克尔和卡隆。 他们用尽各种财政改革方法，但丝 毫不见起色。 最终，路易十六决定做一件自 1614

paragraphs.en: something which had not been done since 1614, he decided to call an Estates General. This would be a great representative assembly that would have the authority make the reforms needed to fix France's finances. As we've mentioned before, in the MiddleAges, people thought of society as divided into 3 different estates: The Church or First Estate, the Nobility or Second Estate, and the Common People or Third Estate. In other words: those who pray, those who fight, and those who work. The estates general was a great representative body that allowed people to vote according to their estate. By 1789 much had changed, but the idea of society divided into 3 estates remained
paragraphs.zh: 年以来就不曾出现的事情，他决定 组建三级议会。 三级议会是一个人数众多的代表 大会，有权为解决法国的财政问题 进行必要的改革。 正如我们之前提到的，在中世纪， 人们认为社会分为三个等级：教会 （第一等级），贵族（第二等级）， 平民（第三等级）。 换句话说，社会上分为：祈祷的人， 战斗的人，工作的人。 三级议会是一个人数众多的代表 机构，允许不同等级的人们投票。 1789 年，社会发生了很大的变化， 但三个不同等级划分的社会观念

paragraphs.en: the same. In the France of 1798 there were about 100,000 Catholic clergy represented by the First Estate, about 400,000 nobility, represented by the Second Estate, and about 25 million commoners represented by the third estate. So the third estate represented about 98% of the population. But in the Estates General they only had about half the vote. The Third Estate got to send 600 delegates while the First and Second Estate got to send 300. Nevertheless, when the Estates General met on May 5th, 1789 there was great excitement and hope that perhaps much needed reforms could be made. But when the Estates General began, the King and the nobility insisted that the
paragraphs.zh: 仍然没有改变。 在 1798 年的法国，大约有 10 万天 主教神职人员代表第一等级，大约 有 40 万贵族代表第二等级，大约 有 2500 万平民代表第三等级。 也就是说，第三等级代表全国 98% 的人。 然而在三级议会中，他们只有一半 的投票权。第三等级有600个代表， 第一、第二等级各有 300 个。 尽管如此，1789 年 5 月 5 日，当三 级议会召开会议时，大家都很兴 奋，也希望进行更多必要的改革。 然而，当三级议会开始后，国王和 贵族坚持各级议会应该作为整体

paragraphs.en: estates should vote as groups. In other words, the 1st estate would get one vote, the 2nd estate one vote, and the 3rd estate one vote. This would mean that the 3rd Estate, representing 98% of the population could be outvoted by the 1st and 2nd estate, representing jut 2%! The 3rd Estate refused to accept this. They insisted that voting should be by delegate (which would give them 600 votes or 50%), not by group (which would only give them 1 vote or 33%) The meeting became divided with the King, most of the nobility, and the upper clergy on one side. The 3rd estate, and some of the lower clergy and liberal nobles were on the other side.
paragraphs.zh: 进行投票。 也就是说，第一等级议会投一票， 第二等级议会投一票，第三等级议 会投一票。 这样就意味着，代表 98%人口的第 三等级议会面对只拥有 2%人口的 第一、二等级议会时，处于劣势。 第三等级议会拒绝接受这一方案。 他们坚持应该由代表投票（这样的 话他们有 600 票，占 50%），而不 是由团体投票（这样的话他们只有 1 票，占 33%）。 会议分成两派，一方是国王，大多 数贵族和上层神职人员。 另一方是第三等级，一些下层神职 人员和自由贵族。

paragraphs.en: The fight over voting continued from May 5th to June 17th. Eventually on June 17th, the 3rd Estate, led by Honore Riqueti, the Compte de Mirabeau, decided to meet separately and call themselves the NationalAssembly. They invited the other two estates to join them, but since the 3rd Estate represented 98% of the country, they should be its legitimate legislature. But on the morning of June 20th, the members of the NationalAssembly found the doors to the meeting hall locked and guarded by soldiers. Fearing that the King was preparing to abolish the NationalAssebmly, the 576 members of the 3rd estates went looking for another meeting place that could hold all of them. On the grounds of Versailles they found
paragraphs.zh: 投票之争从 5 月 5 日持续到了 6 月 17 日。 最终在 6 月 17 日，由米拉波伯爵 ——奥诺雷·里基蒂领导的第三等 级人民，决定单独组织会议，自称 国民议会。 他们邀请了其他两个等级的人加 入，但由于第三等级代表了国家 98%的人，所以他们是实际上的合 法立法机关。 但在 6 月 20 日上午，国民议会成 员发现会议厅的门被锁上了，还有 士兵守卫着。 由于担心国王准备废除国民议会， 第三等级的576名成员开始寻找另 一个可以容纳所有人的集会场所。 他们在凡尔赛宫找了一个室内网

paragraphs.en: an indoor tennis court. There the members of the National Assembly took a collective oath: "Not to separate, and to reassemble wherever circumstances require, until the constitution of the Kingdom is established." This became known to history as the Tennis Court Oath. Many historians view this act of rebellion as the beginning of the French Revolution. On June 27th, the King gave in and allowed the three estates to vote by delegate and not by group. The 3rd Estate had won the first round. Thousands of letters of support began to flow into Versailles from Paris, expressing the support of the people for
paragraphs.zh: 球场开会。 在那里，国民议会成员集体宣誓： “不制定和通过宪法，决不解散。” 这就是众所周知的网球场宣言。 许多历史学家将这次叛乱视为法 国大革命的开始。 6 月 27 日，国王做出让步，允许三 个等级的代表各自投票，不作为团 体投票。 第三等级在第一轮中获胜。 成千上万的支持信件从巴黎源源 不断地寄到凡尔赛宫，表达了人民 对国民议会的支持。

paragraphs.en: the NationalAssembly. But then the King began to move soldiers into the area around Paris. This action made the common people of Paris fear that the King was planning to use force against the NationalAssembly. so on July 14th, the common people rioted in Paris. They decided to attack the Bastille, an old fortress which had long served as a prison and as a symbol of the King's power. After capturing several muskets and cannons the rioters attacked the Bastille and freed the political prisoners being kept there. In the riot the people also killed Bernard René Jourdan the governor of the Bastille as well as Jacques de Flesselles the
paragraphs.zh: 但是，国王开始将士兵转移到巴黎 周围。 这一动作使巴黎平民担心国王计 划对国民议会动用武力。 所以在 7 月 14 号，平民在巴黎闹 事。 他们决定进攻巴士底狱，那是一座 古老的堡垒，长期以来一直是一座 监狱，也是国王权力的象征。 在缴获几支步枪和大炮后，暴乱者 攻占了巴士底狱，释放了关押在那 里的政治犯。 在暴动中，人们还杀害了巴士底狱 总督贝尔纳·勒内·乔丹和巴黎市 长雅克·德·弗莱塞勒。

paragraphs.en: mayor of Paris. The mob then paraded their heads around the city on spikes. The message was clear: the people of Paris were willing to use violence to protect this revolution. The storming of the Bastille frightened King Louis XVI and persuaded him not to use the army. The storming of the Bastille is also the moment when the political struggle between the King and the People turned violent. Today the people of France celebrate July 14th or Bastille Day as their National Holiday. There's also one other important detail: When the Parisians stormed the Bastille, the Parisians wore a red, white and blue
paragraphs.zh: 随后，暴徒们将他们的头绑在铁钉 上在城市里游行。 这一行动传达的信息很明确：巴黎 人民愿意用暴力来保护这场革命。 平民攻占巴士底狱吓坏了国王路 易十六，迫使他无法动用军队。 攻占巴士底狱也表明了国王和人 民之间的政治斗争演变成了暴力。 今天，法国人把 7 月 14 日或巴士 底日作为他们的国庆节。 这里还有另一个重要的细节： 当巴黎人攻占巴士底狱时，巴黎人 戴着红白蓝三色的徽章，这很快成

paragraphs.en: cockade, which quickly became the symbol of the Revolution. And this cockade is the origin of the National Flag of France. But it also inspired peasant revolts which swept across the French countryside over the next 3 weeks from July 17th to August 3rd. Bad weather and bad harvest from the past few years had increased the price of bread and made many of the peasants desperate. The peasants began arming themselves and attacking manor houses. In French history this event is known as the Great Fear. At this point, many of the nobility began to leave France, including the King's own brother. These refugee members of the nobility became known as the emigres.
paragraphs.zh: 为了革命的象征。 这个帽章就是法国国旗的起源。 但它也引发了农民起义，在接下来 的 3 周，从 7 月 17 日到 8 月 3 日， 起义席卷了法国农村。 过去几年的坏天气和歉收促使面 包价格上涨，许多农民对此很绝 望。 农民们开始武装自己，袭击庄园 主。在法国历史上，这一事件被称 为“大恐慌”。 在这一时期，许多贵族开始离开法 国，包括国王的亲兄弟。这些贵族 中的难民被称为流亡者。

paragraphs.en: In order to pacify the enraged peasantry the noble members of the National Assembly renounced their noble privileges. And onAugust 4th, 1789, the National Assembly announced the End of Feudalism. There would be no more serfdom, traditional dues owed to landlords, no special taxation rights and privileges or access to special posts reserved for the nobility. Then the National Assembly went even further, and onAugust 26th, 1789, they issued their Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen. This is perhaps the most famous document to emerge from the French Revolution, and like theAmerican Declaration of Independence it is full of
paragraphs.zh: 为了安抚愤怒的农民，国民议会的 贵族成员放弃了他们的贵族特权。 1789 年 8 月 4 日，国民议会宣布结 束封建统治制度。 废除农奴制，免除传统的欠地主的 租费，贵族不再享有的特殊税收权 利和特权，也不再享有特殊职位。 然后，国民议会更进一步，1789 年 8月26日，他们发表了《人权宣言》。 这可能是法国大革命时期最著名 的文件了，和美国《独立宣言》一 样，它也充满了启蒙思想。它写到:

paragraphs.en: Enlightenment Ideas. It states: - Men are born and remain free and equal in rights. - Their natural rights include "liberty, property, security and resistance to oppression." - Freedom of opinion ("even in religion") - Freedom of the press. - Freedom from arbitrary arrest. - Sovereignty comes from the nation as whole, not the monarchy. This is important because the Declaration of the Rights of Man marks a fundamental change in European thought. For almost 1000 years from 800 until 1789, legitimate power in European politics depended upon your ancestry and relationship to the royal family. Fundamentally that meant how your ancestry traced back to Charlemagne.
paragraphs.zh: ——人类生来自由，权利平等。 ——他们的自然权利包括“自由 权、财产权、安全权和反抗压迫 权”。 ——言论自由(“甚至宗教信仰自 由”) ——新闻自由。 ——免于任意逮捕。 a ——主权来自整个国家，而非君主 政体。 这一点很重要，因为《人权宣言》 标志着欧洲思想从根本上发生了 变化。 从公元 800 年到 1789 年的近 1000 年里，欧洲政治中的合法权力取决 于你的祖先以及你与王室的关系。 从根本上说，这意味着可以追溯到 查理曼大帝时期。

paragraphs.en: But the French Revolution changed all that.After 1789, Europeans increasingly came to accept that political power flowed not from the King and your family, but from the will of the people. This is such a fundamental change in thought, that for many historians, the French Revolution in 1789 marks the end of the Early Modern Period and the beginning of the Later Modern Period, the time we live in now. But we will have to end our lesson here, with the Declaration of the Rights of Man. Because for me this represents the high point of the French Revolution. Afterwards, however, things would take darker turn, and that is the topic of our next lesson. Okay everyone, I hope you enjoyed that lesson and want to learn more.
paragraphs.zh: of 但法国大革命改变了这一切。1789 年以后，欧洲人逐渐接受这一观 念：政治权力不是来自国王和你的 家庭，而是来自人民的意愿。 这是思想上的一个根本性变化，在 许多历史学家看来，1789 年的法国 大革命标志着早期现代的结束、后 现代的开始，也就是我们现在所处 的时代。 现在，我们必须以《人权宣言》结 束我们今天的课程。因为对我来 说，这代表了法国大革命的高潮。 a 然而，在这之后，事情开始变得糟 糕，这就是我们下节课的主题。 好了，同学们，我希望你们喜欢这 节课，并且想了解更多知识。

paragraphs.en: I recommend the podcast Revolutions by Mike Duncan. His third series is about the French Revolution and he goes into great detail about everything that happened. Thank you very much!
paragraphs.zh: 我向你们推荐迈克·邓肯的播客 《革命》。他的第三个系列是关于 法国大革命的，对当时所发生的一 切都做了详尽的描述。 非常感谢!

paragraphs.en: Hello welcome back to our lecture series. Today we will continue the story of the French Revolution. This lesson will focus on the years between 1789 and 1815. We learned last time about the storming of the Bastille on July 14th, 1789. The was followed by the abolition of feudalism in theAugust Decrees. And then the Declaration of the Rights Man and Citizen onAugust 26th. In September the King refused to sign these decrees. Meanwhile, the high price of bread was spreading unhappiness in Paris. This led to a riot by the women of Paris on October 5th, 1789.
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到我们的系列讲 座。今天我们将继续法国大革命的 故事。 这一讲将聚焦于 1789 年到 1815 年 的这个时间段。 上一次我们了解到，1789 年 7 月 14 日，巴士底狱被攻陷了。 随后公布的八月政令废除了封建 制度。 of 然后是 8 月 26 日公布的人权及公 民权宣言。 9 月，国王拒绝签署这些政令。 与此同时，由于面包价格的高昂， 不满情绪也在巴黎蔓延。 这导致了 1789 年 10 月 5 日由巴黎 女性发起的暴动。

paragraphs.en: These women then marched from Paris Versailles with the support of armed revolutionary militia. They carried pikes, muskets and some canons. When the women reached Versailles they overwhelmed the King's guards and took Louis and his family captive. This mob of rebellious women brought the King and his family back to Paris the next day as prisoners. The royal family was kept at the Tuilleries Palace.Adark gloomy medieval palace, much different from the magnificent palace at Versailles. After this the revolutionaries made their next target the Catholic Church in France. On November 2nd, 1789, the National Assembly declared that all Church land
paragraphs.zh: to 在武装革命军的支持下，这些女性 随即从巴黎行军前往凡尔赛。 她们携带了长矛、步枪和一些加农 炮。 当这些女性抵达凡尔赛宫，她们击 败了国王的卫队，俘虏了国王路易 和他的家人。 第二天，这些叛乱的女性将国王和 他的家人作为囚犯带回了巴黎。 皇室家族被拘禁在杜伊勒里宫。 这是一处黑暗阴沉的中世纪宫殿， 与凡尔赛宏伟的宫殿截然不同。 在这之后，革命军的下一个目标是 法国的天主教会。 1789 年 11 月 2 日，国民议会宣布 所有教会的土地如今都归属国有。

paragraphs.en: now belonged to the state. This church property would be used support the value of a new paper currency called the assignat. But then theAssembly began printing assignats and selling church lands to raise money. This decreased the value of the assignat over the next few years, causing economic problems. The next year, on February 13th, 1790 the NationalAssembly abolished all religious orders and monasteries. It passed a law called the Civil Constitution of the Clergy July 12th, 1790. This made every priest an employee of the state. Since it removed his authority, Pope Pius
paragraphs.zh: 教会的财产将被用来支持一种新 的 纸 币 的 币 值 ， 这 就 是 指 券 但随后，国民议会开始印刷指券， 并出售教会土地来增加收入。 这导致在未来的几年里指券发生 了贬值，并导致了经济问题的出 现。 第二年，也就是 1790 年的 2 月 13 日，国民议会废除了所有的宗教机 构和修道院。 1790 年 7 月 12 日，它通过了一项 叫做神职人员民事组织法案的法 律。 宣布所有教士都是国家的雇员。 由于这项法案废除了他的权威，教

paragraphs.en: VI refused to accept this new law. This divided the Catholic church between "constitutional clergy" who swore an oath to work for the government, and the "refractory clergy" who stayed loyal to the pope. It also inspired resistance among Catholics in Brittany, Normandy and the Vendee. Divisions begin to develop in the Assembly, which were reflected in their seating arrangements. The King’s loyalists sat on the right side of the hall; Constitutional monarchists and Nationalists were in the center; And those who wanted to abolish the monarchy were on the left.
paragraphs.zh: 皇庇护六世拒绝承认这项新的法 律。 这将天主教会分成了“立宪教士” 和“反叛教士”两派，前者立誓为 政府工作，后者则继续忠于教皇。 这也激起了布列塔尼、诺曼底和旺 代地区天主教徒的抵抗运动。 分裂开始在国民议会中发展，这反 映在他们的座位安排上。 忠于国王的那些人坐在大厅的右 边。 立宪君主制派和民族主义者坐在 中间。 那些希望废除君主制的人则坐在 左边。

paragraphs.en: This is the origins of our political terms "left" and "right." The most left-wing political group was the Jacobin party, which used a red flag as their symbol. Later on the Socialists and Communists adopted the Jacobin red flag as their own. That's why Communist countries such China and the Soviet Union have red colored flags. In 1790, it looked like France would become a Constitutional Monarchy like England. Two days after the establishment of the Civil Constitution of the Clergy, the nation celebrated the one-year anniversary of the fall of the Bastille. Even the royal family joined the celebrations.
paragraphs.zh: 这就是今天我们的政治概念“左” 与“右”的由来。 其中最左翼的政治团体是雅各宾 派，他们用一面红旗作为自己的象 征。 后来，社会主义者和共产主义者采 纳了雅各宾派的红旗以为己用。 as 这就是为什么如中国和苏联这样 的社会主义国家使用红旗。 1790 年，看上去法国将会成为一个 如英格兰一样的立宪君主制国家 了。 在神职人员民事组织法案通过后 两天，这个国家庆祝了巴士底狱陷 落一周年。连皇室家族也参加了庆 典。

paragraphs.en: France was reorganized from the 39 historic provinces into 83 departments to make administration easier and more rational. France also chose a new flag based on red, white, and blue cockade used at the fall of the Bastille. This Replaced the old Bourbon family flag. But then the King did something very stupid, he began to think about escaping Paris. He wanted to reach the French border town of Montmedy. In Montmedy, a Royalist general named Francois ClaudeAmor commanded an army of 10,000 soldiers who were still loyal to the King. On the night of June 20th, the King and his family escaped from the Tuilleres Palace in disguise.
paragraphs.zh: 法国被从 39 个历史省份重新规划 为 83 个政区，以使行政更加简易 和理性。 法国还选择了基于在攻占巴士底 狱之役中红、白、蓝帽章设计的旗 帜作为国旗，用它代替了旧的波旁 家族旗帜。 但随后，国王做了一些非常愚蠢的 举动，他开始考虑逃离巴黎。 他希望逃往法国边境小镇蒙特梅 迪。 在蒙特梅迪，一位名叫弗朗索瓦·克 劳德·阿穆尔的保皇派统率着一支 一万人的军队，他们依然忠于国 王。 6 月 20 日夜间，国王和他的家人乔 装改扮逃离杜伊勒里宫。

paragraphs.en: They made it as far as the town of Varennes. But in Varennes the King and his family were recognized and captured. They were brought back to Paris under armed guard. This turned public sentiment against the King. Many had believed the King supported the Revolution and reform, but now they suspected him of wanting to lead a counter-revolution. This was a reasonable fear. During the MiddleAges, kings and nobles crushed dozens of peasant uprisings and executed their leaders in awful ways. His "Flight to Varennes" destroyed the King's popularity among the common people.
paragraphs.zh: 他们一直逃到了瓦雷内镇。 但在瓦雷内，国王和他的家人被人 认出，随后被捕。 他们在武装卫士的押送下被送回 了巴黎。 这导致公众情绪开始反对国王。 许多人原本相信国王支持革命和 改革，但现在他们怀疑他是想要领 导一场反革命。 这是合理的担忧，中世纪国王与贵 族们多次镇压过农民起义，并以可 怕方式处决了起义的领导人。 出逃瓦雷内之举摧毁了国王在普 通民众中的支持率。

paragraphs.en: But the NationalAssembly decided to stick by the King, and on July 15th, 1791, it declared that the King would retain his throne in a Constitutional Monarchy. However, radical leaders on the left— Jacques Pierre Brissot, Danton and Camille Desmoullins— argued that the King should be removed from power because of his flight to Varennes. They staged a protest of 50,000 people the Field of Mars on July 17th, 1791. When it turned violent, soldiers under the command of the NationalAssembly opened fire to restore order. The crowd fled, and when it was all over, a few dozen protesters were dead. The King's situation worsened when the Austria’s King Leopold II and Prussia’s King Frederick William II met onAugust 27th, 1791.
paragraphs.zh: 但国民议会决定支持国王，1791 年 7 月 15 日，它宣布国王将在立宪君 主制下维持其王位。 然而，左翼的激进领导人雅克·皮 埃尔·布里索，丹东和卡米尔·德 穆林斯认为，由于逃往瓦雷内之 举，国王应该被取消权力。 at 1791 年 7 月 17 日，他们在战神广 场召集了一场五万人的抗议。 当抗议最终走向暴力，国民议会领 导下的士兵选择了开火以维持秩 序。 群众四散奔逃，当一切尘埃落定， 有数十位抗议者丧生。 1791 年 8 月 27 日，奥地利国王利 奥波德二世和普鲁士国王弗里德 里希·威廉二世举行了会见，这使 得法国国王的处境愈加恶化。

paragraphs.en: Together, they issued the Declaration of Pillnitz. This threatened the invasion of France Louis or his family were harmed. Instead of subduing the French public, aroused angry suspicions that Louis was working with these two foreign kings. Louis XVI's popularity collapsed in the summer of 1791. At the end ofAugust, elections were held for a new LegislativeAssembly to replace the NationalAssembly. These were the first true open elections French history. They lasted fromAugust 29th through September 5th. They returned a fairly moderate assembly, with 345 delegates on the right, 264 in the center and 136 on the left.
paragraphs.zh: 他们一起发表了皮尔尼茨宣言。 其中威胁称，如果路易或他的家人 遭到伤害，就要入侵法国。 it 此举不但没有挟制法国公众，反倒 引起了他们的愤怒猜疑，怀疑路易 十六是在与这两位外国国王串通 一气。 路易十六的支持率在 1791 年夏天 崩溃了。 八月底举行了新一届选举，为了选 出新的立法议会，以取代国民议 会。 这是法国历史上第一次真正的开 放选举，它从 8 月 29 日持续到 9 月 5 日。 最终它形成的是一个相当温和的 议会，代表中有 345 名右翼、264 名中间派和 136 名左翼。

paragraphs.en: In the middle of these elections, on September 3rd, the NationalAssembly completed its work of drafting the Constitution of 1791, which created Constitutional Monarchy under which King had limited powers. Then the National Assembly dissolved itself on September 28th. The LegislativeAssembly took over on October 1st. But things began to go badly in early 1792. Nervous about this French Revolution Austrians and Prussians were organizing a large army near the French borders. This upset the French who demanded that these armies leave. But the Germans gave a non-committal answer.
paragraphs.zh: 在选举过程中，9 月 3 日，国民议 会完成了其起草 1791 年宪法的工 作，这创立了立宪君主制，在其中 a 君主的权力遭到限制。 9 月 28 日，国民议会自行解散。 10 月 1 日，立宪议会接过了权力。 但在 1792 年初，局势开始恶化。 the 因法国大革命而感到紧张的奥地 利人和普鲁士人开始在法国边境 附近集结重兵。 这惹恼了法国人，他们要求这些军 队离开。 但德国人并未答应。

paragraphs.en: Both the King on the right and the politicians on the left thought a war would increase their power. So onApril 20th, 1792, France declared war onAustria and Prussia. But the revolution had disorganized the French army, and its initial moves into Belgium were clumsy and chaotic. The French were defeated in battle. And fled back into France, which caused fear to sweep through the country. Meanwhile, a Prussian-Austrian army formed at the border led by Charles William Ferdinand, the Duke of Brunswick. Brunswick threatened the people of Paris with a manifesto on July 25th, by promising to massacre the Parisians if King or his family were harmed.
paragraphs.zh: 右翼的国王和左翼的政客都认为 战争将增加他们的权柄。 所以在 1792 年 4 月 20 日，法国向 奥地利和普鲁士宣战。 但革命把法国军队搞得一团混乱， 它最初进入比利时的行动杂乱无 章。 法国人于是在战役中一败涂地。 然后他们逃回了法国，这导致恐惧 蔓延到了整个国家。 与此同时，普鲁士-奥地利联军已 经在边境集结，领兵的是布伦瑞克 公爵查尔斯·威廉·斐迪南。 布伦瑞克公爵在 7 月 25 日发表了 一份宣言，威胁巴黎人民说，如果 the 国王和他的家人遭到伤害，他将屠 杀巴黎人。

paragraphs.en: This letter reached Paris onAugust 1st. But instead of scaring the people in enraged them. To them, this was the final proof that the King was working with France's enemies. This letter also convinced the more left-wing revolutionaries that the King must be overthrown to save the Revolution. Four of these men— George Danton, Camille Desmoulins, Jacques Hebert and Maximilien Robespierre— decided to stage a coup. On the night ofAugust 9th they organized a force of armed men at Paris City Hall, the Hotel de Ville. OnAugust 10th these men led an attack on the Tuilleries palace. In the attack they slaughtered the Swiss
paragraphs.zh: 这封信在 8 月 1 日抵达巴黎。 但它并未吓倒人民，而是激怒了他 们。 对他们来说，这是国王勾结法国敌 人的最终证据。 这封信还使得更多的左翼革命军 相信，为了挽救革命，国王必须被 推翻。 其中的四人——乔治·丹敦，卡米 尔·德穆兰，雅克·埃贝尔和马克 西米利安·罗伯斯庇尔——决定发 动政变。 8 月 9 日晚，他们在巴黎市政厅组 织了一支武装力量。 8 月 10 日，这些人发动了一次对杜 伊勒里宫的攻击。 在这次攻击行动中，他们屠杀了保

paragraphs.en: guards who were defending the king. Then these insurgents seized the LegislativeAssembly, declared the end monarchy and the beginning of the Republic. They also called for new elections. The King and his family were imprisoned at the Old Castle of the Medieval Templar Knights. Meanwhile France still had external threats. The German-Austrian army crossed the French border onAugust 19th. It captured Longwy onAugust 23rd and Verdun onAugust 29th. News of these defeats caused great distress in the capital. On September 2nd 60,000 people volunteered to form an army to defend
paragraphs.zh: 卫国王的瑞士卫兵。 然后这些叛乱分子占领了立法议 of 会，宣布了君主制的终结和共和国 的开端。 他们还号召新的选举。 国王和他的家人被关押在中世纪 圣殿骑士团的古堡。 与此同时，法国仍有外部威胁。德 国 - 奥地利军队于 8 月 19 日越过 法国边境。 8 月 23 日，隆维陷落。8 月 29 日， 凡尔登陷落。 这些战败的消息在首都引起了大 范围的悲痛情绪。 9 月 2 日，六万人自愿组成了一支 军队以保卫巴黎。

paragraphs.en: Paris. But at this point, the Revolutionary Jean-Paul Marat advised the volunteers not to leave Paris while the jails were full of counter-revolutionaries who might try to take the city. He called for a "new blood letting." In response, the militia turned into an enraged mob. It began attacking the prisons where hundreds of royalists had been kept since theAugust 10th uprising. Over the next 20 hours a great massacre wound up killing about 1500 people at prisons, hospitals and seminaries around the city. The most famous murder was of Marie Thérèse Louise of Savoy the best friend of Queen MarieAntoinette.
paragraphs.zh: 但就在这时，革命军领导人让 - 保罗·马拉特建议志愿者不要离开 巴黎，此时监狱里到处都是反革命 者，他们可能会试图占领这座城 市。 他号召补充“新鲜血液”。 因此，这支武装力量变成了一伙愤 怒的暴徒。 他们开始攻打监狱，自 8 月 10 日 的政变以后有数百名保皇派被关 押于此。 在接下来的二十个小时里，一场大 屠杀在城市周围监狱、医院和神学 学院中杀掉了 1500 人。 其中最著名的一起谋杀的受害者 是萨伏依的玛丽·特蕾莎·露易丝， 她是王后玛丽·安托瓦内特最好的

paragraphs.en: After a brief show-trial she was beaten death by an enraged mob. The events ofAugust 10th and September 2nd are the moments when the French Revolution took a turn into bloody horror. It got worse. But the French were able to stop the main Prussian army at the Battle of Valmy on September 20th. And they forced the Germans to retreat. On the same day, the Legislative Assembly was replaced by the National Convention. The National Convention then turned its attention to the King. Maximillian Robespierre, now a major leader of the left argued this way: "If the
paragraphs.zh: 朋友。 to 在一场简短的表演性质的审判之 后，她被一名愤怒的暴徒活活打 死。 8月10日和9月2日的事件标志着 法国大革命转向血腥恐怖的时刻。 局势恶化了。 但法国人仍成功地在 9 月 20 日的 瓦尔密战役中阻止了普鲁士军队 的主力部队。 然后他们迫使德国人撤了军。 同一天，立法会议被国民公会所取 代。 国民公会随即将注意力转向了国 王。 如今已经是左翼主要领导人的马 克西米利安·罗伯斯庇尔这样说：

paragraphs.en: King is not guilty, then those who have dethroned him are." On December 3rd, 1792, King Louis XVI was put on trial for treason against the Nation. The trial lasted from December 3rd to January 17th, 1793. On January 17th, the National Convention voted to execute the King. Four days later, on January 21st, 1793, Louis XVI was beheaded. This killing horrified the kingdoms of Europe. And it united them against Revolutionary France. The Holy Roman Empire, Prussia, Great Britain, Spain, the Dutch Republic, Portugal and various Italian states allied against Revolutionary France.
paragraphs.zh: “如果国王没有罪，那么那些推翻 他的人就有了。” 1792 年 12 月 3 日，国王路易十六 因叛国罪出庭受审。 审判从 12 月 3 日持续到了 1793 年 的 1 月 17 日。 1 月 17 日，国民公会表决决定处决 国王。 四天以后，1793 年 1 月 21 日，路 易十六被送上断头台。 这场处决震骇了其他欧洲王国。 并使得他们团结起来对抗革命法 国。 神圣罗马帝国、普鲁士、大不列颠、 西班牙、荷兰共和国、葡萄牙和多 个意大利王国结成同盟，反对革命 法国。

paragraphs.en: Within France, the more conservative parts of the country were horrified by the attacks on the church and the killing of the King. Arebellion broke out in the Vendee region of France. But the French were able to do something that had never been done before. They issued the "Levee en Masse", a universal conscription of all young men into the army. With the Levee en Masse the Revolutionary French were able to raise an army of 1.5 million men and fight off all their enemies. Unlike their opposing monarchs, the French Revolutionaries could call upon the full strength of the nation and its people. But It was still a chaotic and dangerous
paragraphs.zh: 在法国内部，这个国家最保守的地 区被攻击教会、处决国王之举吓坏 了。 法国旺代地区爆发了一场叛乱。 但法国人现在能够做一些以前从 未做过的事情。 他们发布了 “Levee en Masse”，这是征集所 有年轻人进入军队的普遍征兵制 度。 在这种普遍征兵制下，革命法国得 以召集起了一支 150 万人的军队， 击退了它的全部敌人。 和他们的对手君主制国家不同，法 国革命军可以调动起国家和它的 人民的全部力量。 但对于共和国而言，这依然是一个

paragraphs.en: time for the Republic. There was a rebellion in the Vendee and invasion from outside. In this time period, the hard-left faction, led by Maximillian Robespierre and George Danton, seized control of the government and led the country from January 1793 until July 1794. This period from September 1792 until July 1794 is known as the Reign of Terror. During this time, the hard-left faction of revolutionaries killed the King's friends and supporters in the September massacres. Then they killed the King himself in January 1793. At the same time, Robespierre and his allies began to go after their fellow revolutionaries.
paragraphs.zh: 混乱且危险的时期，旺代发生了叛 乱，外部还面临侵略。 正是在这一时期，由马克西米利 安·罗伯斯庇尔和乔治·丹敦领导 的左翼强硬派控制了政府，并在 1793 年 1 月到 1794 年 7 月这段时 间里领导着国家。 从 1792 年 9 月到 1794 年 7 月的这 段时间被称为恐怖统治。 在此期间，革命军的左翼强硬派在 九月的多次屠杀中杀掉了国王的 朋友和支持者。 然后他们又在 1793 年 1 月处决了 国王本人。 与此同时，罗伯斯庇尔和他的盟友 们开始转向他们的革命军同袍。

paragraphs.en: Amore moderate group of Revolutionaries called the Girondins were arrested in a coup on May 31st, 1793. The Girondins were imprisoned. Then they were executed in October 1793. The hard-left faction, led by Robespierre, killed other members of the nobility all through 1793, and any members of the political right. They killed Queen MarieAntoinette on October 16th, 1793. Agruesome new machine became a symbol of this revolutionary period. The Guillotine was a heavy blade on a track that could cut off a human head in an instant.
paragraphs.zh: 一个被称为吉伦特派的更加温和 的革命派团体在 1793 年 5 月 31 日 的一场政变中被逮捕。 吉伦特派被囚禁了。 然后他们在 1793 年 10 月被处决 了。 罗伯斯庇尔领导的左翼强硬派在 整个 1793 年杀掉了贵族阶层的其 他成员，还有所有政治上的右派。 他们在 1793 年 10 月 16 日杀掉了 王后玛丽·安托瓦内特。 一台令人毛骨悚然的新机器成为 这个革命时期的象征。 断头台是在一条轨道上装配的沉 重刀刃，可以瞬间切下人的头。

paragraphs.en: It had been invented by Dr. Joseph Ignace Guillotin, and it was designed to kill quickly. The original purpose had been humane. Guillotine wanted to make execution less painful by replacing death by torture, which was common in the middle ages. But the Guillotine might have made killing a little too easy, since around 20,000 people were beheaded during the reign of terror. Towards the end, the radical left even turned on itself. Robespierre had George Danton executed onApril 5th, 1794. By the summer of 1794 Robespierre's power began to make many members of the National Convention nervous. He was overthrown on July 27th, 1794, in an event called the Thermidorian Reaction. Robespierre was himself beheaded the
paragraphs.zh: 它是由约瑟夫-伊尼亚斯·吉约丹 博士发明的，目的是尽快杀死犯 人。 最初的目的是人道的。 断头台希 望通过取代折磨致死来减少死刑 中的痛苦，而折磨致死在中世纪很 常见。 但断头台使得杀戮有一点太容易 了。在恐怖统治时期，大约有两万 人遭到斩首。 最后，激进左翼们甚至转向了他们 自己。罗伯斯庇尔在 1794 年 4 月 5 日处决了乔治·丹敦。 到 1794 年夏天，罗伯斯庇尔的权 力开始让国民公会中的很多成员 感到紧张。1794 年 7 月 27 日，他 在一次史称热月政变的事件中被 推翻。 第二天，也即 7 月 28 日，罗伯斯

paragraphs.en: next day on July 28th. From July 28th 1794 until November 2nd, 1795, French politics was in a state of internal chaos as different factions struggled for power. But at least the reign of terror was over. Eventually a group of five men called the Directory managed to seize power. They led France from November 2nd, 1795, until November 10th, 1799. Meanwhile wars with outside countries continued. During this time a young artillery officer named Napoleon Bonaparte began to show leadership ability. First at the Siege of Toulon in late 1793. On October 5th, 1795, Bonaparte put down a royalist uprising in Paris and
paragraphs.zh: 庇尔自己也被送上了断头台。 从 1794 年 7 月 28 日，到 1795 年 11 月 2 日，法国政治处于内部混乱 之中，不同的派别为争夺权力而斗 争。 但至少，恐怖统治结束了。 最后，一个被称为督政府的五人团 体成功掌握了权力，他们在从 1795 年 11 月 2 日到 1799 年 11 月 10 日 的这段期间领导法国。 与此同时，与外国的战争仍在继 续。 在这段时间，一位年轻的名叫拿破 仑·波拿巴的炮兵军官开始显露出 领导能力。 首先是在 1793 年底的土伦港围城 战。 1795 年 10 月 5 日，波拿巴镇压了 一场巴黎的保皇派起义，捍卫了督

paragraphs.en: defended the Directory. The Directory then rewarded him with the ItalianArmy in March of 1796. This army was viewed as the least important because it was poorly supplied and on the least important front of the war. But Bonaparte reorganized it and it to remarkable victories in 1796 and 1797. In 1797 and 1798 he led a campaign in Egypt which was intended to cut off the British supply lines with India. Then Bonaparte returned to France in August of 1799 where he joinedAbbe Sieyès in a plot against the Directory. The two of them overthrew the Directory and seized power in a coup d'etat on November 9th, 1799. On November 10th, 1799, a new
paragraphs.zh: 政府。 督政府于是在 1796 年 3 月用一支 意大利军队奖励了他。 这支军队被认为是最不重要的，因 为它装备短缺，也位于战争最不重 要的阵线。但波拿巴重新组织了这 led 支军队，并在 1796-1797 年带领它 取得了令人瞩目的一系列胜利。 在 1797 到 1798 年，他领导了一次 在埃及的军事行动，目的是切断英 国与印度之间的补给线。 随后，在 1799 年 8 月，波拿巴返 回了法国，并参加了西哀士神父发 动的一场反对督政府的图谋。 这两个人在 1799 年 11 月 9 日的一 场政变中推翻了督政府并掌握了 权力。 1799年11月10日，一个被叫做“法

paragraphs.en: government called "The French Consulate" was formed. In theory three men led the country and shared power. But in reality Bonaparte was able to seize more and more power for himself. On May 18th, 1804, Bonaparte had become so powerful that the French Senate declared him Emperor. The Consulate period ended and the French Empire began. Here is a list of all the French governments in the 15 years between Louis XVI and Napoleon. On December 2nd, 1804, Bonaparte had himself crowned by the Pope in a grand ceremony at Notre Dame Cathedral. Bonaparte was a brilliant general, and under him, France was constantly at war with the other military powers.
paragraphs.zh: 兰西执政府”的新政府成立了，理 论上，有三个人领导着这个国家并 分享权力。 但实际上，波拿巴已经能够越来越 多地为他自己攫取权力。 1804 年 5 月 18 日，波拿巴的权力 已经如此之大，乃至法国参议院将 他宣布为皇帝。 执政府时代结束，法兰西帝国开始 了它的历史。 这是从路易十六到拿破仑的十五 年，法国历任政府的名单。 1804 年 12 月 2 日，波拿巴在巴黎 圣母院一场盛大的仪式上由教皇 加冕。 波拿巴是一位杰出的将领，在他的 领导下，法国持续与其他军事力量 进行着战争。

paragraphs.en: During the Revolutionary period, France fought the wars of the First and Second Coalitions. Under Napoleon it fought wars with the other European powers in the Third, Fourth, Fifth and Sixth Coalition. Napoleon smashed these enemy armies great battles such asAusterlitz where Napoleon destroyed the Russian and Holy Roman Empire armies; the Battle of Jena where he destroyed the PrussianArmy; And the Battle of Friedland, where he defeated the Russians for a second time. Due to Napoleon's brilliance, France had conquered most of Europe and its power reached its greatest height. But through it all the British refused to make peace.
