paragraphs.zh: 在革命军时期，法国与第一和第二 同盟军进行了战争。 在拿破仑的领导下，它又与其他结 成第三、第四、第五和第六同盟军 的欧洲国家进行了战争。 in 拿破仑在数次大战中摧毁了这些 敌军，比如奥斯特利茨，拿破仑在 那里摧毁了俄罗斯帝国和神圣罗 马帝国的军队。 耶拿之战，其中他摧毁了普鲁士的 军队。 还有弗里德兰战役，他在那里第二 次击败了俄罗斯人。 由于拿破仑的杰出能力，法国征服 了欧洲大部分地区，其力量达到了 最高峰。 但在此过程中，整个不列颠都拒绝 和谈。

paragraphs.en: And they defeated Napoleon's navy at Battle of Trafalgar. This ensured that Britain maintained control of the High seas. Now it became the “Battle of the Whale vs. the Elephant,” with the British dominant at sea and the French on land. But Napoleon made mistakes. In 1807, got into a long and costly war in Spain, which drained his empire of men and money. Napoleon called the war "The Spanish ulcer" The French executed those Spanish who rebelled, but this just increased resistance towards them. Bonaparte made his biggest mistake in 1812. He invaded Russia. The French army won many battles, but
paragraphs.zh: the 他们在特拉法加战役中击败了拿 破仑的海军。 这确保了英国保持对公海的控制。 如今此役被称为“鲸象之战”，英 国握有海上的主导权，法国人则统 治了陆地。 he 但拿破仑也犯下了一些错误。1807 年，他在西班牙卷入了一场漫长而 代价高昂的战争，这抽干了他的帝 国的人力与财力。 拿破仑称这场战争为“西班牙溃 疡”。 法国人处决了那些反叛的西班牙 人，但这只加剧了针对他们的抵抗 运动。 波拿巴在 1812 年犯下了他的最大 的错误。他入侵了俄罗斯。 法国军队赢得了很多战役，但他们

paragraphs.en: they were weakened by cold, disease, desertion and attrition. And eventually they had to retreat. Bonaparte assembled an army of 680,000 soldiers to invade Russian. During that invasion he lost over 500,000 men. This invasion destroyed France's power. Asixth coalition was formed against France. Bonaparte was defeated at the Battle of Leipzig on October 19th, 1813. After this defeat he was exiled to the Island of Elba off the coast of Italy. But the next year Bonaparte escaped from Elba and returned to France. Soldiers sent to arrest him, switched sides and joined him instead. This led to one of history's most amazing
paragraphs.zh: 被寒冷，疾病，遗弃和消耗所削弱。 最终他们不得不撤退。 为了入侵俄罗斯，波拿巴集结了 68 万人的军队。在这场入侵战争中他 损失了超过 50 万人。 这次入侵摧毁了法国国力。 第六次反法同盟形成了。 波拿巴在 1813 年 10 月 19 日的莱 比锡战役中被击败。 在这次战败之后，他被流放到了意 大利海外的厄尔巴岛。 但第二年，波拿巴逃离厄尔巴岛， 返回了法国。 士兵被派去逮捕他，但最后反而倒 戈加入了他的麾下。 这带来了史上最激动人心的反攻

paragraphs.en: comeback stories. Bonaparte almost regained control. But he was defeated at the Battle of Waterloo on July 8th, 1815. And then Bonaparte was exiled to the remote island of St. Helena, where he had no possibility of escape. There Bonaparte lived out the rest of his life. He died on May 5th, 1821. Meanwhile the victorious powers had a great meeting called the Congress of Vienna to plan out the future of Europe. But we will leave the politics and war aside. Because while all of this turmoil was happening as a result of the French Revolution, an even more important change was beginning to happen in England.
paragraphs.zh: 故事之一。波拿巴几乎重新赢得了 控制权。 但在 1815 年 7 月 8 日，他在滑铁 卢战役中被击败。 然后波拿巴被流放到了遥远的圣 赫莱纳岛，在这儿他再也没有可能 逃离了。 波拿巴在这里度过了他的余生。 他死于 1821 年 5 月 5 日。 同时，战胜而归的诸国召开了一次 被称为维也纳会议的大会，以谋划 欧洲的未来。 但我们将把政治和战争放在一边。 因为在所有这些暴乱作为法国大 革命的结果接连发生的时候，一场 甚至更加重要的变化正发生在英 格兰。

paragraphs.en: It was the beginning of the Industrial Revolution.And that is the topic of our next class. Okay everyone, I hope you enjoyed that lesson and I have two recommendations for you. Again I recommend The Revolutions Podcast. And I also recommend the Epic History Youtube channel and their series on Napoleon Bonaparte. Thank you very much!
paragraphs.zh: 这就是工业革命的开端。这将是我 们下一讲的内容。 好了，我希望你们喜欢这一讲，另 外我还有两项推荐。 再一次地，我还是要推荐播客《革 命》(非官方)。 然后也推荐 Youtube 频道《历史史 诗》(非官方)以及他们关于拿破 仑·波拿巴的系列。谢谢你们！

paragraphs.en: Hello and welcome back to our lecture series on World History. Today we will learn about the Industrial Revolution. Our lesson will focus on the years between 1690 and 1830, here is what that looks like on our timeline. We will begin this lesson by thinking about energy. So far in this class we have looked at thousands of years of human history. We have learned about great civilizations, lives of kings and queens, religious movements, great wars, discoveries and art. In other words, the stuff of history. But one thing has remained unchanged during all that time, 90% of the human race was busy with farming. In his book Sapiens the writer Yuval
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到我们的世界历史 系列讲座。今天我们将要了解到的 是工业革命。 我们的这一讲将会集中在 1690 年 到 1830 年的时间段，这是它在我 们时间线上看起来的样子。 我们将以对能源的思考来开始这 一课。 到目前为止，在本课程中，我们已 经了解了数千年的人类历史。 我 the 们了解了伟大的文明，国王和王后 的生活，宗教运动，伟大的战争， 发现和艺术。 换句话说，那些构 成历史的东西。 但有一件事始终不变，90%的人类 都忙于农耕。 在他名为《智人》的著作中，作家

paragraphs.en: Noah Hariri put it well, writing: "History is something that very few people have been doing while everyone else was ploughing fields and carrying water buckets." And during all that time, the basic way that people farmed did not change very much. For example, in 2200 BC, theAncient Egyptians plowed their land with animal power. And 4000 years later in 1800AD, people were still doing the same thing. And that's because for 99% of human history all of our energy has come from the sun. The sun gave plants energy to grow and drove the winds and water cycle. We burned those plants for fuels, we ate
paragraphs.zh: Yuval Noah Hariri 很清楚地说出 了这一点：“历史是那些只有很少 人一直在做的事情，而其他人都在 耕田和运送水桶。” 而一直以来，人们耕作的基本方式 都没有发生太大改变。 比如说，公元前 2200 年，古埃及 人在用畜力犁他们的田。 在四千年以后的公元 1800 年，人 们仍在做同样的事。 这是因为，在 99%的人类历史当中， 我们的全部能源都来自于太阳。 太阳给植物以生长的能量，并推动 大气和水循环。 我们焚烧那些植物作为燃料，我们

paragraphs.en: and our animals ate them for our muscle power, we used falling-water to power water wheels and we relied on the wind for our sailboats. The power for our entire civilization relied upon each year's solar energy. And one of the biggest sources of energy was wood. We have been burning wood since the stone age to keep us warm and cook our food. Wood is humanity's oldest fuel source. Later we learned how to convert wood into charcoal to provide a hotter burning fuel. This charcoal could be used to make metal, to make glass and in other industrial processes. But as our population grew, we needed more and more wood, so we cut down
paragraphs.zh: 和我们的动物食用这些植物来增 加肌肉力量，我们用下落的水流来 推动水车，我们依赖风力来推动帆 船。 我们的全部文明的能量都依赖于 每年的太阳能。 而最大的能源之一是木材。 自石 器时代以来，我们一直在燃烧木 材，让我们保持温暖，烹饪食物。 木材是人类最古老的燃料来源。 后来我们学会了如何将木材转化 为木炭，以提供更热的燃料。 这种木炭可用于制造金属，制造玻 璃并应用于其他工业过程。 但随着人口的增长，我们需要越来 越多的木材，因此我们砍伐了越来

paragraphs.en: more and more of the forests. We also cleared the forests to make room for more and more farmland. This was particularly true in Europe. As a result, Europe became increasingly deforested. There was less and less wood to go around. On the left is a map of Europe's forest cover in 1000 BC, and on the right is the forest cover in 1500AD. By that point most of the ancient forests were gone. People turned to other techniques for wood production. They began growing trees like hazel which can be cut again and again in a technique called coppicing. This produces a regular supply of wood each year. Here is an example of a freshly coppiced tree, and that same tree a year later.
paragraphs.zh: 越多的森林。 我们还清理了林地，为越来越多的 农田腾出空间。 这在欧洲尤其如此。 结果，欧洲变得越来越荒芜。 周 围的木材越来越少了。 左边是公元前 1000 年欧洲森林覆 盖的地图，右边是公元 1500 年的 森林覆盖情况。 此时，大多数古 森林都消失了。 人们转向其他木材生产技术。 他 们开始种植榛子之类的树木，这种 树木可以通过一种称为“矮林平 茬”的技术一次又一次地切割。这 每年都会产生定期的木材供应。 这是一棵刚被修剪过的树的例子， 和一年以后的同一棵树。

paragraphs.en: But even with such techniques the Europeans in the late middle ages began to experience an energy shortage. This was especially true in England, which is after all, an island. It was around this time that people began to pay more attention to strange black rocks which wash ashore on beaches, especially to the north in Scotland. These rocks were special because you could burn them for fuel like wood or charcoal. So people called these rocks: "sea coal" People had been burning sea coal in Britain since at least the Roman period, but in the 1500s and 1600s the shortage of firewood created a renewed interest this strange black rock. Today we know that sea coal is caused
paragraphs.zh: 但即使有了这样的技术，中世纪晚 期的欧洲人仍然遭遇了能源短缺。 尤其是在英格兰，这毕竟是一个岛 屿。 大约就是在这个时候，人们开始更 加关注在海滩上冲上岸的奇怪的 黑色岩石，特别是在苏格兰的北 部。 这些岩石很特别，因为你可以点燃 它们作为燃料，就像木材或者木炭 一样。 所以人们将这些石头称为“海煤”。 至少从罗马帝国时代起，生活在不 列颠的人们就开始燃烧海煤了，但 在 16 和 17 世纪，燃料的短缺使人 们对这些奇怪的黑色石头产生了 新的兴趣。 今天我们知道，当煤层被暴露在海

paragraphs.en: when a seam of coal is exposed to the ocean like in this picture. Over time the action of waves and tides will break off little pieces of coal, which then wash a-shore. So collecting this sea coal and selling it became a minor industry and was part the English economy until the 20th century. That's what these people are doing in this painting Sometimes the coal seam comes to the surface on the land as well.And people soon figured out that these rocks were same as sea coal. So in the 1500s and 1600s, the English first began digging mines to go after the stuff. These first mines were primitive, just a
paragraphs.zh: 中时就会产生海煤，如图所示。 随着时间的推移，波浪和潮汐的运 动将把煤层打成小块，然后冲上岸 边。 因此，收集这种海煤并出售它成了 of 一个小型产业，直到 20 世纪都是 英国经济的一部分。 图中这些人正在做这件事。 有时煤层也会在陆地上露出来。人 们很快就发现这些岩石和海煤一 the 样。 所以在 16 和 17 世纪，英国人首先 开始挖矿来寻找这些东西。 最初的矿井非常原始，只是地上的

paragraphs.en: hole in the ground that gradually expanded outwards into a bell-shape. And these early mines soon ran into problems, as they went deeper into the earth, the mine often went below the water line. Once this happened the mine would slowly fill up with water, and no more coal could be extracted. Many coal mines were also near the ocean. And they also faced similar problems with flooding. Of course people tried to pump the water out of the mine. They developed better and better pumps, and used horses to provide the power. They called these pumps "Engines" or "Gins" and here is an illustration of one
paragraphs.zh: 一个洞，逐渐才向外发展为钟形。 这些早期煤矿很快惹上了麻烦，因 为当人们向地下挖得更深，煤矿常 常会低于水线。 一旦这种情况发生，煤矿就会慢慢 被水充满，再也不会有更多的煤被 开采出来。 还有许多煤矿地近海边。 它们也面临着类似的灌水问题。 当然人们试图将水泵出矿井。 他 们开发出更好的泵，并用马来提供 动力。 他们将这些泵称为“Engines”或 “Gins”，这里是一个图解。

paragraphs.en: of them. These horse-pumps helped, but their power was limited, and they could only pump out water to a depth of 30 meters. Any coal deeper than that was inaccessible. But the 1600s was also the age of science in England, and in the 1690s a scientist named Denis Papin became fascinated with the idea of using steam to drive a piston. Eight years later, another scientist named Thomas Savery thought up the idea of using the partial vacuum created by boiling water to create a steam-powered pump. His 1689 steam pump was the world's first device designed to move water using steam.
paragraphs.zh: 这些马泵有所帮助，但它们的动力 有限，它们只能抽出 30 米深的水。 所有埋藏得比这个更深的煤都无 法获得。 但17世纪也是英格兰的科学世纪， 在 17 世纪 90 年代，一位名叫丹尼 斯·帕潘的科学家对使用蒸汽驱动 活塞的想法着了迷。 八年后，另一位名叫Thomas Savery 的科学家产生了利用沸水产生的 局部真空来制造蒸汽动力泵的想 法。 他的 1689 年蒸汽泵是世界上第一 台使用蒸汽来移动水的设备。

paragraphs.en: But it didn't work very well. It required huge amount of fuel to move even a tiny amount of water. The real break-through came 14 years later in 1712, when a man named Thomas Newcommen invented the world's first steam engine. Newcommen's steam engine was a combination of the steam pump and the steam piston. Instead of using the vacuum of steam to move water, Newcommen realized that you could use the steam to drive a piston, which would pump the water directly. First, Water is boiled in a large tank. Second, a valve is opened. Third, steam pushes the piston up... .. and further up... Until at the top, the valve to the boiler
paragraphs.zh: a 但它效果不佳。即使是非常少量的 水，它也需要大量的燃料来移动。 真正的突破在十四年以后来临，也 就是 1712 年，当时一个名叫托马 斯·纽克曼的人发明了世界上第一 台蒸汽发动机。 纽克曼的蒸汽机是蒸汽泵和蒸汽 活塞的组合。 他没有使用蒸汽真 空来抽取水，而是意识到你可以用 蒸汽来驱动活塞，活塞可以直接抽 水。 首先，水在一个大水槽中被煮沸。 然后，一个阀门被打开。 第三，蒸汽向上推动活塞...... 继续推动…… is 直到顶部，通往煮水槽的阀门关上

paragraphs.en: closed. Then cold water is injected to cool the boiler down. The steam condenses back into liquid water and the piston falls... ... and falls ... Until the piston reaches the bottom of the cyclinder... ... and the cycle begins again. Newcommen's steam engine was powerful it could pump as much water 20 horse pumps, and it could pump out water from a much greater depth. But the engine consumed massive amounts of coal. This was fine at a coal mine where coal was very, very cheap. But the Newcommen engine was too expensive to use anywhere other than a
paragraphs.zh: 了。 然后冷水被注入水槽，以降低其温 度。 蒸 汽 冷 凝 回 液 态 水 ， 活 塞 下 继续下降…… 直到活塞到达气缸底部...... ……然后循环再次开始。 纽克曼的蒸汽发动机功能强大，它 as 可以泵送 20 马泵的水量，也可以 从更大的深度泵出水。 但这种发动机需要消耗大量的煤 炭。 这在煤非常非常便宜的煤矿 中不成问题。 但它太贵了，不能用于煤矿以外的 任何地方。

paragraphs.en: coal mine. So for about 60 years, the only steam engines in the world were at British coal mines. But this was still a big change, because coal represents the earth's stored solar energy. Coal is ancient wood that became submerged in a swamp, hundreds of millions of years ago. Then over time, that wood was put under pressure as new layers of earth were deposited on top of it. The pressure, heat and millions of years first turned the wood into peat, and then turned the peat into coal. The steam engines allowed the British start using more and more coal, which was important because 1 kilogram of coal contains as much energy as 1.75
paragraphs.zh: 所以在大约 60 年的时间里，世界 上仅有的蒸汽发动机全在英国煤 矿里。 但这依然是一个很大的改变，因为 煤代表了地球上储存的太阳能。 煤是古老的木材，几亿年前就被淹 没在沼泽中。 然后随着时间的推移，当新的土层 沉积在其上时，木材就受到了压 力。 压力，热量和数百万年的时 间首先将木材变成泥炭，然后将泥 炭变成煤炭。 蒸汽机使得英国人能够开始使用 越来越多的煤，这很重要，因为 1 公斤煤含有相当于 1.75 公斤柴火 的能量。

paragraphs.en: kilograms of firewood. This allowed the British to greatly expand their energy supply. Let's think about wood to understand this better. This is a modernAmerican football field. And this is the size of an EnglishAcre, about 4046 square meters. Now let's imagine an acre of forest. Now each year you might want to harvest wood from that forest, but you cannot cut down too many trees or your forest will die. It turns out that an acre of forest can sustainably produce about 1200 kilograms of wood per year. That 1200 kilograms of wood contains about 4.8 million kilocalories of energy, which is the same as 685 kilograms of
paragraphs.zh: 这使英国能够大大扩展其能源供 应。 为了更好地理解这一点，让我们考 虑一下木材的情况。这是一个现代 的美式橄榄球场。 or 这是一英亩的大小，约 4046 平方 米。 现在我们想象一下一英亩的森林。 现在每年你都可能想要从那片森 林中采伐木材，但你不能砍伐太多 的树木，否则你的森林就会死亡。 事实证明，一英亩的森林每年可持 续生产约 1200 公斤的木材。 1200公斤的木材含有约480万千卡 的能量，与 685 公斤的煤相同。

paragraphs.en: coal. In 1850, British coal mines produced 72 million tons of coal, thanks to steam engines. That's the same amount of energy as the firewood from 105 million acres of forest. To put that in perspective Britain only 51 million acres of land. And in 1850 it only had 3 million acres of forest land and 26 million acres of farmland. That means Britain's coal mines produced 35 times as much energy as all of its forests. The steam engine and coal mining caused a 35-fold increase in the energy available to the British economy. And while that was happening, Britain's
paragraphs.zh: 1850 年，由于蒸汽机，英国煤矿生 产了 7200 万吨煤。 这与 1.05 亿英亩森林的木柴能量 相同。 has 考虑一下，英国只有 5100 万英亩 的土地。 1850 年，它只有 300 万英亩的林地 和 2600 万英亩的农田。 这意味着英国的煤矿产能是其所 有森林的 35 倍。 蒸汽机和煤炭开采使英国经济可 用的能源增加了 35 倍。 在这种情况发生的同时，英国最大

paragraphs.en: largest industry was undergoing a transformation. Ever since the MiddleAges, woolen cloth production had been Britain's leading industry. It was about 50% of GDP and 75% of exports. To make woolen cloth first you have to shear the sheep. Second, you must card the wool. Third, you must spin the wool into thread. Fourth, you must weave the thread into cloth. Then you have to pound the cloth to make more full. Finally, a tailor can sew the cloth into clothes.
paragraphs.zh: 的产业正在经历转型 自中世纪以来，羊毛布生产一直是 英国的主导产业。 它占 GDP 的 50％左右，占出口的 要制作羊毛布，你必须先剪羊毛。 其次，你要梳理这些羊毛。 第三，你要将羊毛纺成线。 第四，你要将线织成布。 然后你要捶打布，使其更饱满。 最后，裁缝可以将布料缝制成衣 服。

paragraphs.en: This system is at least 7000 years old, going all the way back to the domestication of sheep. In the 1200s, there were two major improvements to this system. First the spinning wheel was invented around 1280, which was much faster than the drop spindle. Second, people started using water-powered mills to beat the wool with heavy hammers instead of fulling hand. Anyway, by 1700 weaving and spinning were two very important professions in the English economy. Traditionally, it took 3 spinners on spinning wheels to produce enough thread for 1 weaver on a traditional loom. But all that began to change in 1733
paragraphs.zh: 这个系统至少有 7000 年的历史， 一直可以追溯到绵羊的驯化。 13 世纪，这个系统中产生了两个重 要改进。 首先，在 1280 年左右发明了纺车， 它比落轴更快。 其次，人们开始使用水动力磨机用 重锤击打羊毛，而不是手工捶打。 无论如何，到 1700 年，编织和纺 纱已经是英国经济中两个非常重 要的行业。 传统上，一台纺车需要三个纺线工 人来为一名传统织机上的织工生 产足够的线。 但所有这一切在1733年开始改变，

paragraphs.en: when a man named John Kay invented tool called the "Flying shuttle". The flying shuttle moved the thread back and forth on the loom just by pulling a cord. Before then, two people had to toss the thread to each other while the weaver worked. With a flying shuttle, 1 weaver could now do the work of 6 people using the traditional method. This greatly increased weaving productivity. But now, with the increased speed of the flying shuttle, it took 18 spinners to supply 1 weaver. The demand for spinner's increased. This problem was solved by a man named Robert Hargreaves. He created a type of spinning wheel that
paragraphs.zh: a 当时一个名叫约翰·凯的人发明了 一种名为“飞梭”的工具。 只需拉动绳索，飞梭就可以在织机 上前后移动。 在此之前，在织工 工作时，必须有两个人反复将线轴 交给对方。 在飞梭的帮助下，一名织工现在可 以完成传统方法下六个工人的工 作量。 这大大提高了织布行业的生产力。 但现在，有了飞梭所增加的速度， 需要 18 个纺线工人来供应一个织 工。 对纺线工人的需求量增加了。 这个问题是由一个名叫罗伯特·哈 格里夫斯的人解决的。 他创造了一种可以同时旋转多个

paragraphs.en: could spin multiple threads at once. He named this device the "Spinning Jenny" in honor of his daughter Jennifer. Later versions would allow one spinner to manage up to 120 different threads. But the first version of the Spinning Jenny could spin as much thread as 8 traditional spinning wheels. There was a disadvantage, Spinning Jenny thread was not a strong as Spinning wheel thread. But it was 8 times cheaper! This was still useful because woven cloth has two types of threads: the warp threads which run up and down, and the weft threads which run left and right. The warp threads need to be strong, because they are under tension in a loom.
paragraphs.zh: 线轴的纺车。 他将这个装置命名为“珍妮纺纱 机”，因为他的女儿名叫珍妮弗。 这个装置后来发展出的版本将能 够允许一个纺线工人最高同时管 理 120 个不同的线轴。 但珍妮纺纱机的首个版本就能管 理 8 台传统纺车的线轴总量。 有一个缺点：珍妮纺纱机纺出的线 不如传统纺车的线那样强韧。 但它便宜八倍！ 这仍然是有用的，因为编织布有两 种类型的线：上下运行的经线和左 右延伸的纬线。 经线需要很强韧，因为它们在织机 中处于张紧状态。 所以织工一直

paragraphs.en: So weavers kept using spinning wheel thread for those. But the weft thread doesn't need to be strong, so the cheaper spinning Jenny thread was used instead. Four years later, another inventor named RichardArkwright created the Water Frame. This was basically an improved version of the Spinning Jenny. But the water frame could be driven by water power instead of by a person. This allowed the process to scale up dramatically. And it allowedArkwright to create the world's first textile factory in 1776. Three years later in 1779, another innovator named Samuel Crompton invented the Spinning Mule. Now a worker with a Spinning Mule
paragraphs.zh: 在使用纺车轮线。 但纬线不需要 很强韧，所以可以使用更便宜的珍 妮纺纱机纺出的线来代替。 四年后，另一位名叫理查德·阿克 赖特的发明家创造了水力纺纱机。 这基本上是旋转珍妮的一个改进 版本。 但水力纺纱机能够使用水力驱动， 而不是人力。这使得生产过程得以 大规模扩张。 它使得阿克赖特在 1776 年创建了 世界上第一家纺织工厂。 三年后的 1779 年，另一位名叫塞 缪尔·柯朗普敦的发明家发明了纺 纱骡子（走锭细纱机）。 现在，一名使用纺纱骡子的工人将

paragraphs.en: could produce as much thread as 48 workers with Spinning Wheels. And the thread was better quality and stronger than the spinning wheel thread. The spinning wheel was now obsolete. This shows us how great innovations really happen. Each inventor builds on the ideas of those who came before. Now the problem was reversed from where it had been in 1733. Now the spinners could produce faster than the weavers, even with flying shuttle looms. There was now a demand for more weavers. And 5 years later, the problem was solved by Edmund Cartwright, when he invented the Power Loom. Meanwhile, steam engines were getting better.
paragraphs.zh: 能生产出相当于使用传统纺车的 48 名工人的生产量。 并且它生产的线也比传统纺车纺 出的线质量更好，更强韧。 纺车现在已经过时了。 这张图向我们展示了伟大的创新 是如何真正发生的。每一个发明家 都建立在前人想法的基础上。 现在这个问题与 1733 年的情况相 反了。现在，即使使用飞梭织机， 纺纱厂的生产速度也比织布机快。 现在需要的是更多的织布工人。 五年后，埃德蒙·卡特赖特解决了 这个问题，当时他发明了动力织 机。 与此同时，蒸汽发动机也变得更加 完善。

paragraphs.en: In 1763 a young mechanic named James Watt was working in a tool making shop and was given the job of repairing a small model version of the Newcommen engine. As Watt took the model apart he carefully studied how it worked, and Watt noticed something. Newcommen's engine was very inefficient. For the next two years, Watt carefully studied the machine, trying to see if he could make it better. And he found a way. Watt realized that by heating and cooling the steam in the same cylinder, Newcommen's engine was wasting about 3/4s of its power. So Watt developed a new kind of steam engine that separated the boiler, piston and condenser.
paragraphs.zh: 1763 年，一位名叫詹姆斯·瓦特的 年轻机械师正在一家工具制造商 店工作，工作内容是修理纽克曼发 动机的一个小型模型版。 当瓦特将模型分解开来时，他仔细 研究了它是如何工作的，然后他注 意到了一些东西。 纽克曼发动机效率很低。 在接下来的两年里，瓦特仔细研究 了这台机器，想看看他能否改进 它。 他找到了办法。 瓦特意识到由于 加热和冷却同一气缸中的蒸汽，纽 克曼的发动机浪费了大约3/4的功 率。 因此瓦特开发了一种新型蒸汽机， 将锅炉，活塞和冷凝器分开。

paragraphs.en: Watt's steam engine was a revolution! It could do the same work as Newcommen's engine, but it used only 1/4 of the fuel. But Watt had trouble solving several technical challenges and getting his machine to market. This is when Watt met his most important partner: a manufacturer named Matthew Boulton. Boulton already owned a factory called the Soho Manufactory which produced small metal toys and objects. Boulton ran his factory on water wheel power, but during a drought these could not run. So Boulton wanted to see if a steam engine could power his factory instead. He asked Watt if his engine could do that, and Watt said yes, but it needed some
paragraphs.zh: 瓦特的蒸汽机是一场革命！ 它可 以做与纽克曼发动机相同的工作， 但它只使用了 1/4 的燃料。 但是 Watt 在试图解决几个技术挑 战并将他的机器推向市场时遇到 了麻烦。 正在此时，瓦特遇到了他最重要的 合作伙伴：一个名叫马修·布尔顿 的制造商。 布尔顿已经拥有一家名为 Soho Manufactory 的工厂，该工厂生产 小型金属玩具和产品。 布尔顿用水轮驱动他的工厂，但在 干旱期间，这些都无法运行。 所以布尔顿想看看蒸汽机是否可 以为他的工厂供能。 他问瓦特他的发动机是否可以做 到这一点，瓦特说是的，但它还需

paragraphs.en: work. Boulton's metal workers were the perfect people to help Watt finish developing his engine. Boulton and Watt went into business together.And they solved several more problems to produce the first steam engine efficient enough to be used outside of coal mines. And again, this steam engine was not the result of one genius, but of many innovators building on each other's ideas. Progress continued into the 19th century. In 1797, an inventor named Richard Trevithick created the first high-pressure steam engine. The high-pressure steam engines were smaller with a greater weight to power ratio. So over the next ten years, people
paragraphs.zh: 要一些工作。 布尔顿的金属工人是帮助瓦特完 成发动机开发的完美人选。 布尔顿和瓦特一起开展了业务。 他们解决了另外几个问题，生产出 足以在煤矿外使用的第一台蒸汽 机。 而且，这台蒸汽机不是一个天才的 思考成果，而是许多创新者建立在 彼此的想法上的共同结晶。 进展持续到了 19 世纪。 1797 年，一位名叫理查·特里维西 克的发明家创造了第一台高压蒸 汽机。 高压蒸汽发动机体积更小，重量与 功率比更大。 因此，在接下来的 十年里，人们开始将它们放入船和

paragraphs.en: began putting them into boats and carts, to make the first steam boats and steam locomotives. And in the 1830s, the railroad was born. As was the age of the steam ship. Progress in textile production continued with two inventions by Richard Roberts: Robert's loom and the SelfActing Spinning Mule. Textile production was now hundreds of times faster than it had been a century before. As the technology improved, the number of looms in Britain increased 100 fold during the first half of the 19th century. Putting all these innovations together: textiles, steam engines, railroads, shipping as well as others in metallurgy, electricity and chemistry, and it is clear
paragraphs.zh: 推车中，制造出第一代蒸汽船和蒸 汽机车。 十九世纪三十年代，铁路诞生了。 这是属于蒸汽船的时代。 纺织品生产的进展由理查德·罗伯 茨的两项发明继续推动着：罗伯特 织机和自动旋转骡子。 纺织品的生产现在比一个世纪前 快了数百倍。 随着技术的进步，英国的织机数量 在 19 世纪上半叶增加了 100 倍。 将所有这些创新汇合在一起：纺 织，蒸汽机，铁路，航运以及其他 在冶金，电力和化学领域的创新发 a 明，显然新的时代已经开始：工业

paragraphs.en: new era had begun: The Industrial Revolution. And this Revolution made human beings astonishingly more wealthy. Within 6 generations the average citizen in an industrialized country was 100 times richer than his or her ancestors. And this wealth allowed for innovations in other areas, which caused a population explosion. This revolution began in Britain, but then began spreading into the rest of Europe during the rest of the 1800s. And it set the stage for the tumultuous 19th century, which is the topic of our next class. Okay everyone I hope you enjoyed that lesson and want to learn more. I have some recommendations for you.
paragraphs.zh: 革命。 而这场革命让人类变得令人惊讶 地富有。 在六代人之内，工业化国家的普通 公民变得比他或她的祖先富裕 100 倍。 这笔财富也为其他领域的创新创 造了条件，导致了人口爆炸。 这场革命始于英国，但在 19 世纪 的其他时间里开始蔓延到欧洲其 他地区。 它为动荡的 19 世纪奠定了基础， 这是我们下一堂课的主题。 好了，我希望大家喜欢这一课，并 希望了解更多。 现在我有一些推 荐。

paragraphs.en: The first is the book Sapiens by Yuval Noah Hariri. The second is the website How Stuff Works, which has great explanations of how steam engines work. And the third recommendation is the Documentary series Connections with James Burker. This is a Documentary series from 1978 which explores the way one idea leads another throughout history. Episode 6 discussed the Steam Engine, but all of them are good. Thank you all very much!
paragraphs.zh: 首先是尤瓦尔·赫拉利的这本书， 《人类简史》。 然后是一个网站，“事物是如何运 转的”(非官方)，它为蒸汽机如何 工作给出了详尽的解释。 第三个推荐是詹姆斯·伯克的系列 纪录片《关联》(非官方)。 这是开始于1978年的系列纪录片， 探讨了一个想法在整个历史中是 如何引领另一个想法的。 其中的第六集讨论了蒸汽机，但整 个系列都很赞。 谢谢大家！

titleZh: 第十九讲：漫长的十九世纪

paragraphs.en: Hello and welcome back to our lecture series, today we will be learning about the "long" 19th century. The "long" 19th century stretches from the beginning of the French Revolution 1789 and the start of World War One in 1914. Since these two events had such a big impact on European history, the Russian author Ilya Ehrenburg believed that they were the natural start and end points for period known as the "long 19th century" In our previous classes we learned about the French Revolution and its impact on Europe. But the French Revolution also had impacts on other parts of the world, specifically the French colony of San
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到我们的系列讲 座，今天我们将要了解到的是“漫 长”的十九世纪。 “漫长”的十九世纪是从 1789 年 in 法国大革命开始，一直延伸到 1914 年第一次世界大战爆发的这段时 间。 由于这两起事件对欧洲历史产生 了如此之大的影响，俄罗斯作家伊 利亚·爱伦堡相信，它们是“漫长 的十九世纪”这段历史时期天然的 起点和终点。 在我们之前的课程当中我们已经 了解过了法国大革命和它对于欧 洲的影响。 但法国大革命对于世界的其他部 分同样产生了影响，特别是法国殖 民地圣多米尼克，也就是现在的海

paragraphs.en: Domingue, now the nation of Haiti. Haiti was an island dominated by slave plantations and sugar farms. So in 1789, when the French began talking about liberty, equality and brotherhood these ideas began making their way to the slaves on Saint Domingue. This combined with a weakened central government inspired the slaves to rebel. Led by the ex-slave Toussaint Louverture the slaves overthrew the government on Saint Domingue and won their freedom. They then renamed the island Haiti. This all happened in 1791. In 1799 Napoleon became the leader of France. First as the consul and later as emperor. Then in 1802 Napoleon decided to
paragraphs.zh: 地。 海地是一个岛屿，上面主要是奴隶 种植园和蔗糖农庄。 所以在 1789 年，当法国人开始谈 论自由、平等和友爱，这些概念也 渗入了圣多米尼克的奴隶们心中。 这些，加上当时衰弱无力的中央政 府，激起了奴隶的反叛。 在前奴隶杜桑·卢维杜尔的领导 下，奴隶们推翻了圣多米尼克政 府，赢得了他们的自由。 然后他们将这个岛屿重新命名为 海地。这一切都发生在 1791 年。 1799 年，拿破仑成为法国领导人， 起初是执政，后来成了皇帝。 然后在 1802 年，拿破仑决定重新

paragraphs.en: re-enslave the blacks on Haiti and use the profits from sugar to pay for his wars. So he sent an army under Charles Leclerc to re-enslave the island. Leclerk sailed for Haiti with a fleet and French army in 1802. But the Haitians fought back ferociously and defeated Leclerc's army. This preserved their independence and liberty. But Napoleon still had a large chunk of land in the middle of NorthAmerica called "Louisiana." But without Haiti Louisiana was worthless to Napoleon. So he offered to sell the entire territory to theAmericans for 68 million Francs. (This would be worth about 600 billion dollars today) This was a very good deal for the
paragraphs.zh: 征服海地岛上的黑奴，并用蔗糖贸 易的利润来支付他的战争成本。 因此，他派出了一支军队，由查尔 斯·勒克莱尔领导，目的是重新奴 役该岛。 a 勒克莱尔带领一支舰队和一支法 国陆军在 1802 年渡海前往海地。 但海地人进行了猛烈抵抗，击败了 勒克莱尔的军队。这保护了他们的 独立和自由。 但拿破仑仍然在北美中部拥有一 大片土地，叫做“路易斯安那”。 然而没有海地，路易斯安那对拿破 仑来说毫无价值。所以他准备以 6800 万法郎的价格把整块地卖给 美国人。（在今天这笔钱大约相当 于 6000 亿美元。） 对于美国人来说这是一笔非常好

paragraphs.en: Americans and President Jefferson and the US Congress agreed. This was the United States before the Louisiana purchase. And this was the country afterwards. The territory of the United states doubled overnight. Having given up on Haiti, Napoleon Bonaparte next targeted Spain in 1807. In 1807 he invaded both Spain and Portugal, which launched the Peninsular war of 1807. But Spain at that time also controlled a vast empire overseas in theAmericas. With the mother country under French occupation, the Spanish colonies in America all began to rebel. By 1830, the Spanish empire had broken up into more than a dozen independent
paragraphs.zh: 的交易，所以杰弗逊总统和美国国 会都同意了。 这是购买路易斯安那之前的美国。 这是之后的美国。美国的领土范围 在一夜之间翻了一倍。 在放弃了海地以后，拿破仑·波拿 巴在 1807 年瞄准了西班牙。 1807 年，他同时入侵了西班牙和葡 萄牙，开始了 1807 年半岛战争。 但当时的西班牙仍在美洲控制着 庞大的海外帝国。 由于宗主国被法国占领，美洲的西 班牙殖民地全都开始了反叛。 到 1830 年，西班牙帝国已经分裂 成了十几个独立国家。

paragraphs.en: nations. And many of these nations, such as Mexico, outlawed slavery, or at least made it illegal for anyone to be born a slave. This began a long process of abolition across theAmericas. Meanwhile, in the northern part of the United States more and more states began outlawing slavery. They were inspired by the ideals of liberty created by theAmerican Revolution, and slavery was not a big part of their economies. But in the south slavery persisted. So by 1800 the northern states were free, but the southern states protected the slave system. At this point, in the late 1700s and early 1800s a new social movement arose which was dedicated to ending slavery.
paragraphs.zh: 其中的许多国家，比如墨西哥，立 法废除了奴隶制，或者至少是宣布 生而为奴的规定非法。这开启了美 洲漫长的废除奴隶制的进程。 与此同时，美国北部也有越来越多 的州立法禁止了奴隶制。 他们受到了美国革命所创造的自 由理想的启发，并且奴隶制并不是 他们经济的重要组成部分。 但在南方仍维持着奴隶制。所以， 到 1800 年，北方州已经解放，但 南方州仍在捍卫奴隶制系统。 在这个时候，在 18 世纪末和 19 世 纪初，一场旨在终结奴隶制的新的 社会运动兴起了。

paragraphs.en: This was the abolitionist movement, and its members were called abolitionists. Here are some of the early leaders of the movement. In England, they managed to outlaw the Atlantic slave trade in 1807. And in 1833 they abolished slavery in entire British empire. This was also the period of the early industrial revolution, as we learned last time. And by the early 1790s, Britain was beginning to industrialize. But this also created a problem. British factories had become so productive, that they started running out of wool. Europe just could not raise enough sheep to meet demand. Fortunately for the English factories an
paragraphs.zh: 这就是废奴运动，其成员被称为废 奴主义者。这是其部分早期领导 人。 在英格兰，他们成功地在 1807 年 宣布大西洋奴隶贸易非法。 the 1833 年，他们在整个大英帝国废除 了奴隶制。 正如我们上一次已经了解到的，这 一时期同时也是工业革命的早期。 到十八世纪九十年代初，英国开始 工业化。 但这也带来了一个问题。英国工厂 生产力变得如此之高，以至于羊毛 被耗尽了。欧洲养不了多到能够满 足需求的羊。 英国工厂十分幸运，一位名叫 Eli

paragraphs.en: American inventor named Eli Whitney solved this problem with a new machine called "the cotton gin" You see before cotton can be made into fabric, the fluffy part, called the lint, must be separated from the seed. Traditionally this was done by hand, but it is a slow process. Eli Whitney's cotton gin could separate the seeds and the lint much, much faster. By hand, a worker could produce about 1/2 kilogram of cotton lint a day. But a worker with a cotton gin could produce 25 kilograms a day, a 50-fold increase! This made cotton much, much cheaper, so in the 1790s, theAmerican south could start supplying the British factories with their raw materials.
paragraphs.zh: Whitney 的美国发明家用一种名为 “轧花机”的新机器解决了这个问 题。 你可以看到，在将棉花制成织物之 前，被称为棉绒的蓬松部分必须与 种子分开。 传统上这是靠手工完成的，但这非 常慢。 Eli Whitney 的轧花机能够迅速得 多地分开种子与棉绒。 如果依靠手工，一名工人一天可能 能生产 0.5 千克棉绒。 但有了轧花机，一名工人一天将能 生产 25 千克棉绒，提高了 50 倍！ 这极大地降低了棉花的价格，因此 在 1790 年代，美国南方可以开始 向英国工厂供应原材料。

paragraphs.en: The British altered their machines to work with cotton instead of wool, and industrial revolution accelerated. In the northern parts ofAmerica, businessmen began to copy the British and created their own textile factories. The southern cotton farmers sold cotton to them too. But all of this cotton was grown on farms worked by slaves. Now that the cotton crop was so profitable, the slave system in the American south became stronger and stronger. From 1793 until 1865, slavery became inextricably linked to cotton production. During this same periodAmericans began moving west to settle the new Louisiana territory.
paragraphs.zh: 英国人改造了他们的机器，使其能 the 够处理棉花而不是羊毛，工业革命 由此加速。 在美国北方，商人开始模仿英国创 立他们自己的纺织工厂。 南方的棉农们也卖棉花给他们。 但是所有这些棉花都是在奴隶工 作的农庄里种植的。 既然棉花作物如此有利可图，美国 南部的奴隶制度变得越来越强大。 从 1793 年到 1865 年，奴隶制变得 与棉花生产密不可分。 与此同时，美国人开始向西迁移， 去新领土路易斯安那定居。

paragraphs.en: Of course there were already Native Americans living in that land. But the WhiteAmericans had the advantage of numbers, technology and diseases, and they wiped out the NativeAmericans and pushed them off their land. In the north the settlers tended to be families who were moving west to start their own farms. But in the south it tended to be slave owners who moved west with their slaves to start new cotton farms. This is map ofAmerica in 1800. And here is the country in 1821.As you can see slave states and free states moved west together. Then in 1846, theAmericans got into a war with their southern neighbor Mexico. This was pushed by southern politicians who were looking for new land in the
paragraphs.zh: 当然，已经有美洲原住民在那片土 地上生活。 但美国白人拥有数量， 技术和疾病的优势，他们消灭了美 洲土著，将他们赶出了这片土地。 在北方，移居者倾向于组成家庭， 然后向西迁移，开辟自己的农庄。 但是在南方，往往是奴隶主带着自 己的奴隶迁往西部，开辟新的棉花 农庄。 这是 1800 年的美国地图。 这是 1821 年的美国。正如你所见， 蓄奴州和自由州都在向西扩张。 然后在 1846 年，美国人与他们的 南方邻国墨西哥爆发了一场战争。 这是由南方政客推动的，他们正在 南方为了蓄奴农庄寻找新的土地。

paragraphs.en: south for slave farms. The war lasted from 1846 to 1848 and Americans won. They even captured Mexico's capital city. In the end Mexico was forced to surrender half of its territory. This land later became theAmerican states of Texas, Colorado, Utah, New Mexico, Arizona, Nevada and California. This map shows usAmerica in 1821. And here isAmerica in 1858. The country now stretched across the entire NorthAmerican continent. But it was increasingly divided over the issue of slavery. In 1860 the issue of slavery became so divisive that it split the major political parties and four men ran for president. The anti-slavery candidateAbraham
paragraphs.zh: the 战争从 1846 年持续到了 1848 年， 美国人赢了。他们甚至占领了墨西 哥首都。 最终墨西哥被迫割让了它的一半 领土，这块土地后来成了美国的德 克萨斯、科罗拉多、犹他、新墨西 哥、亚利桑那、内华达和加利福尼 亚州。 这张地图显示了 1821 年的美国。 而这是 1858 年的美国。这个国家 如今横跨了整个北美大陆，但却在 奴隶制问题上日益分裂。 1860 年，奴隶制问题变得如此至关 重要，因为正是它将主要政党以及 四名总统候选人区分了开来。 反蓄奴的候选人亚伯拉罕·林肯

paragraphs.en: Lincoln said that he would not touch slavery in the states where it already existed, but he also said he would not allow slavery to spread to the territories. And without getting any votes in the deep south,Abraham Lincoln won the presidency in November 1860. That's because the northern free states had grown faster over the last 80 years and had much bigger populations and economies. The Election ofAbraham Lincoln enraged the southern states, who refused to accept an anti-slavery northerner as President. So in the months between Lincoln's election and inauguration, 11 states voted to leave the United States and form a new country that would protect slavery: The Confederate States ofAmerica.
paragraphs.zh: 说，他不会去动那些业已存在的蓄 奴州，但他也说他不会允许奴隶制 在美国领土上扩张。 在没有获得来自南方腹地的任何 选票的情况下，亚伯拉罕·林肯在 1860 年 11 月当选美国总统。 这是因为北方自由州在过去的八 十年里发展如此之快，已经拥有了 多得多的人口和经济总量。 亚伯拉罕·林肯的当选触怒了南方 州，他们拒绝承认一个反蓄奴的北 方人是总统。 所以在林肯当选到就职的几个月 里，十一个州投票决定离开美利坚 合众国并组成一个保护奴隶制的 新国家：美利坚联盟国。

paragraphs.en: Unfortunately for them, theAmerican Army still controlled a fort on an island near Charleston South Carolina. The fort was called Fort Sumter, and the American army refused to evacuate it. So onApril 12th, 1861, the Confederate army attacked the fort, and theAmerican Civil War began. The war lasted for four years, and it was a battle between the Northern United States and the Southern Confederate States. It was a long and bloody war, but the north won in the end. OnApril 9th, 1865 the main Southern General, Robert E. Lee, surrendered to the main Northern General, Ulysses S. Grant. And at the same time,Abraham Lincoln
paragraphs.zh: 但对他们来说不幸的是，美国陆军 仍控制着南卡罗来纳州查尔斯顿 附近的一个岛上的堡垒。 这座堡垒叫做萨姆特堡，美国军队 拒绝从中撤出。 所以，在 1861 年 4 月 12 日，联盟 军队攻击了这个堡垒，美国内战由 此爆发。 这场战争持续了四年，这是一场北 方的美利坚合众国与南方的美利 坚联盟国之间的战斗。 战争过程漫长而血腥，但最终北方 赢得了胜利。 1865 年 4 月 9 日，南方主将 Robert E. Lee 向北方主将 Ulysses S. Grant 投降。 与此同时，亚伯拉罕·林肯成功地

paragraphs.en: was able to get Congress to pass the 13th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution. This made slavery illegal once and for all. When theAmendment was finally passed by the House of Representatives, the congressmen burst into cheers. And similar joy was felt as the news of liberation spread to the southern slaves. Unfortunately just 5 days after the war ended,Abraham Lincoln was shot in the head while watching a play. The Assassin, John Wilkes Booths, hated blacks and was enraged by the end slavery. After being shot in the head, the men in the audience carried the wounded Lincoln out of the theater. They placed him in a bed in a boarding
paragraphs.zh: 使得国会通过了美国宪法的第十 三修正案。这意味着奴隶制从此成 为非法。 当修正案最终在参议院经代表投 票通过，许多国会议员爆发出了欢 呼。 当自由的消息传到南方奴隶中间， 同样的喜悦也被他们所分享。 不幸的是，战争结束后仅仅五天， 亚伯拉罕·林肯就在观看一场戏剧 时被子弹击中头部。 刺客名叫 John Wilkes Booths，他 of 痛恨黑人，并且被废除奴隶制一事 激怒了。 在林肯被击中头部以后，观众席上 的男性立刻将受伤的林肯送出了 剧院。 他们将他放在了街对面一间叫做

paragraphs.en: house across the street called Peterson house.And there, doctors tried to save him. But the wound was too serious, and Lincoln died the next morning around 7am. He ended slavery and saved the country, but he paid for the salvation ofAmerica with his own life. By 1865 slavery was gone, but the western part of the country was still unsettled. But 47 years later the entire west was settled, and theAmerican economy was on its way to becoming the largest in the world. Meanwhile, major events continued to happen in Europe. Napoleon suffered his final defeat at the
paragraphs.zh: 彼得森住所的公寓床上。医生赶到 这里来抢救他。 但伤势太严重了，林肯在第二天早 上七点左右去世。 他终结了奴隶制，拯救了国家，但 他为了拯救美国而付出了自己生 命的代价。 到 1865 年，奴隶制已经终结，但 国家西部仍荒无人烟。 但四十七年以后，整个西部人烟稠 密，美国经济开始向世界最大经济 体发展。 与此同时，欧洲仍在发生重大事 件。 拿破仑在 1815 年 6 月 18 日的滑铁

paragraphs.en: Battle of Waterloo on June 18th, 1815. After Napoleon lost power, he was replaced by Louis XVIII and then Charles X, two younger brothers of Louis XVI. They brought back the old Bourbon flag, and ruled France for the next 15 years. But then another revolution happened in July of 1830. And the Revolutionaries forced Charles X to abdicate and the crown went to his more liberal cousin Louis Philippe I. Louis Philippe ruled France for 18 more years until yet another period of revolutions began in 1848. These revolutions began in France, but then they swept across Europe, challenging all of the ancient monarchies. 1848 was a momentous year in European
paragraphs.zh: 卢战役中遭受了他的最终失败。 在拿破仑丧失了权柄以后，取代他 的是路易十八，以及随后的查理十 世，都是路易十六的弟弟。他们带 回了旧的波旁家族旗帜，并统治了 法国接下来的十五年。 但 1830 年 7 月，另一场革命爆发 了。 革命军迫使查理十世退位，王位传 给了他更加自由主义的表兄弟路 易·菲利普一世。 路易·菲利普又统治了法国十八 年，直到 1848 年，新一段革命时 期开始。 这些革命运动开始于法国，但它们 随即横扫了欧洲，所有的古老君主 制国家都遭到了挑战。 1848 年是欧洲历史上重要的一年，

paragraphs.en: history, full of liberal revolutions, socialist revolutions, nationalist movements, small wars, and counter revolutions. It was also in this year that Karl Marx published the Communist Manifesto and the Communist movement was born. But in the autumn of 1848, most of the revolutionary movements and nationalist movements were crushed as the old empires and kings re-asserted control. In France, however, the revolution forced Louis Philippe, the last King of France resign. His power was given to the NationalAssembly, and the "Second Republic" was born on February 26th, 1848. That December the French held their first ever election for president. But instead of supporting the liberals, the
paragraphs.zh: 期间自由主义革命，社会主义革 命，民族主义运动，小型战争和反 革命运动接连爆发。 这也是卡尔·马克思发表其《共产 党宣言》，以及共产主义运动诞生 的那一年。 但在 1848 年秋天，大多数革命运 动和民族主义运动都被镇压，旧帝 国和国王们重新掌握了控制权。 然而在法国，革命迫使法兰西的最 to 后一位国王路易·菲利普下了台。 他的权力被交给了国民议会，“第 二共和国”在 1848 年 2 月 26 日诞 生。 那年的 12 月，法国人举行了他们 有史以来第一次总统选举。 但法国人民并没有支持自由派，而

paragraphs.en: people of France voted overwhelmingly in favor of a man named Louis Napoleon Bonaparte III. Napoleon the III was the nephew of the great Napoleon Bonaparte. Here is his family tree. But Napoleon III had the same ambition to be great that had driven his uncle on. From 1848 to 1851 he shared power with the national assembly. But then in 1851, Napoleon III staged a coup. Napoleon overthrew the National Assembly and declared himself Emperor. Napoleon ruled France during the next years, and under his rule France fully entered the Industrial Revolution. Napoleon III also had ambitious plans remake Paris with the help of his
paragraphs.zh: 是以压倒性态势将选票投给了一 个叫做路易·拿破仑·波拿巴三世 的人。 拿破仑三世是那位伟大的拿破 仑·波拿巴的侄子。这是他的家谱。 但拿破仑三世也有同样的、曾驱动 过他的叔叔的那种变成伟人的野 心。从 1848 年到 1851 年，他与国 民议会分享着权力。 但在 1851 年，拿破仑三世发动了 一起政变。 他推翻了国民议会，自称皇帝。 拿破仑在接下来的 22 年里统治着 法国，在他的统治下，法国完全进 入了工业革命时代。 to 在 他 的 建 筑 师 George Eugene Haussmann 的帮助下，拿破仑三世

paragraphs.en: architect George Eugene Haussmann. Their goal was to enlarge the city, widen the streets and improve the infrastructure. In 1851, Paris was still very much a medieval city. It looked much the way it had in the 1500s and 1600s. He named this device the "Spinning Jenny" in honor of his daughter Jennifer. Haussmann changed all that. He tore down old buildings, widened the streets, and made the city look more like this. Or like this. Today when people think of Paris, they often imagine the wide Boulevards and elegant high rises on either side. Not realizing that this Paris is a much more modern version of the city. Haussman and Napoleon III also
paragraphs.zh: 也有重建巴黎的宏伟计划。 他们的目标是扩大城市，拓宽街道 并改善基础设施。 1851 年，巴黎仍是一个非常中世纪 的城市。 它看上去与它在 16、17 世纪时没 有什么区别。 城市充斥着黑暗狭窄的街道，像这 张图上显示的那样。 Haussmann 彻底改变了这一切。他 拆除建筑，拓宽街道，然后让城市 看上去更像是这样。 或是这样。 今天当人们想到巴黎，他们常常想 象宽阔的林荫大道和两边优雅的 高楼，而没有意识到这个巴黎是这 座城市一个远为现代的版本。 Haussman 和拿破仑三世还改善了

paragraphs.en: improved the city's parks, they took royal garden and made them public, as well as upgrading parks that already existed. But another benefit of the wide avenues was that they made crowd control much easier. The barricades of 1830 or 1848 would be impossible in this new Paris. Meanwhile other major events were happening elsewhere in Europe. Aperiod of Italian Unification began in 1848 and lasted until 1871 when the country finally unified with Rome as its capital. And in the 1860s Prussia began to absorb more and more of Germany under the leadership of the "Iron Chancellor" Otto Von Bismarck. Bismarck increased Prussian territory through clever diplomacy and short decisive wars with Denmark andAustria.
paragraphs.zh: 城市的公园，他们接收了皇家花园 并向公众开放了它们，并对已经存 在的公园进行了升级。 但宽阔街道的另一个好处是它们 使控制人群变得更加容易。 在这 个新的巴黎，1830 年或 1848 年式 的封锁是不可能的。 与此同时，重大事件也发生在欧洲 的其他地区。 意大利的统一开始于 1848 年，并 持续到了 1871 年，当时这个国家 终于统一，并定都罗马。 十九世纪六十年代，普鲁士开始在 “铁腕首相”俾斯麦的领导下逐步 吸收德意志。 俾斯麦通过灵活的外交手段，以及 与丹麦及奥地利的简短但具有决 定性的战争，扩大了普鲁士领土。

paragraphs.en: By 1870, Prussia and all of north Germany were unified into the North German Confederation. But many southern German states were still independent. So Bismarck picked a fight with France, and goaded the over-confident Napoleon III into declaring war. And in July 1870, the Franco-Prussian war began. But while the French were stronger on paper, the Prussians were much better prepared for war. Their soldiers were better trained, better equipped and better led. They defeated 3 French armies in battle. And captured their men, forcing the French into a humiliating surrender.
paragraphs.zh: 到 1870 年，普鲁士与整个德意志 北部统一到了北德意志联邦当中。 但许多位于德意志南方的州仍处 于独立状态。 所以俾斯麦选择了与法国开战，并 在宣战时谴责过度自信的拿破仑 三世。 在 1870 年 7 月，法兰西-普鲁士战 争爆发。 尽管法国人在理论上应当更加强 盛，但普鲁士的战争准备要充分得 多。他们的士兵训练有素、装备精 良，也有得当的指挥。 他们在战斗中击败了三支法国军 队。 并俘虏了他们的士兵，迫使法国人 屈辱地投降。

paragraphs.en: In fact, Napoleon III was even captured in this debacle. Then the Prussians marched on Paris and put it under a 4-month siege. Paris finally surrendered in Early February. The Prussians held a victory march through the city, forced the French to pay a huge amount of money, and took two border provinces. Then they left. And Bismarck was able to use the war spirit and common enemy of France to persuade the southern German states to join the union. On January 18th, 1871 Bismarck announced the formation of the German Empire. For the first time in history, the Germans were now unified into a single strong state. This completely upset the balance of power in Europe.
paragraphs.zh: 事实上，甚至连拿破仑三世也在这 次惨败中被俘了。 随后普鲁士人进军巴黎，并对之进 行了四个月的围城。 巴黎最终在 2 月初投降。普鲁士人 在城市中进行了胜利游行，强迫法 国人支付了高昂财物，并夺走了两 个边境省份。然后他们离开了。 俾斯麦成功地用战争精神与共同 的敌人法国说服了南方的德意志 诸邦，使他们加入了同盟。 1871 年 1 月 18 日，俾斯麦宣布建 立德意志德国。 史上第一次，德意志人统一到了一 个强大的单一国家之中，这完全打 破了欧洲的权力平衡。

paragraphs.en: Here is a map of Europe in 1871 after the German unification. 1870 also began a new period of industrialization involving steel, petroleum, automobiles, electricity and chemicals. Some historians call this the Second Industrial Revolution. At the same time the Europeans began push their way into the interior ofAfrica. And by 1913 they had colonized the entire continent. Meanwhile, as new empires were forming, an old one was falling apart. The old Ottoman Empire had been a super-power in the 1500s and 1600s, but it reached its peak around 1683. After that Ottoman power began a long slow decline throughout the 1700s and
paragraphs.zh: 这是德国统一之后，1871 年的欧洲 地图。 1870 年也开始了涉及钢铁，石油， 汽车，电力和化学品的新工业化时 期。 有些历史学家将它称为第二次工 业革命。 与此同时，欧洲人开始向非洲腹地 推进。 到 1913 年，他们殖民了整块大陆。 与此同时，当新的帝国开始形成， 旧的帝国正在分裂。旧奥斯曼帝国 在 16 和 17 世纪曾是超级强国，但 它在1683年左右达到了它的巅峰。 自那以后，在整个 18 和 19 世纪， 奥斯曼帝国的权力开始了漫长的

paragraphs.en: 1800s. In 1821 their first great loss occurred with the War of Greek Independence. For 8 years the Greeks fought a war against the Ottomans and with British support they succeeded. This is a map of the Balkan peninsula around 1830. Next Turkey lost a war with Russia from 1877 to 1878. Awhole group of new nations broke free at that point. And the Ottomans were driven out of the Balkans entirely in 1913. This is a map of the Balkans in 1913. But now theAustro-Hungarian Empire and the Russian Empire began to covet this territory.
paragraphs.zh: 缓慢衰落。 1821 年，他们在希腊独立战争中遭 遇了第一次重大失利。 在八年时间里，希腊人在英国人的 帮助下与奥斯曼帝国进行了长期 战争，并且他们成功了。 这是 1830 年左右巴尔干半岛的地 图。 然后土耳其在1877-1878年与俄罗 斯的战争中战败。 此时，一批新国家获得了自由。 1913 年，奥斯曼帝国彻底退出巴尔 干。 这是 1913 年的巴尔干地图。 但现在奥匈帝国和俄罗斯帝国开 始觊觎这块领土。

paragraphs.en: In 1908, theAustro--Hungarians seized Bonsia and Herzegovinia. This enraged the Serbs next door. You see Serbian nationalists believed that Bosnia should be part of much bigger Serbian state called "Greater Serbia" So around this time a Serbian terrorist group formed to achieve this goal. They were called the "Black Hand" And then in 1914, the crown prince of Austro-Hungary -Franz Ferdinand - decided to pay a visit to Bosnia with his wife Sophie. In late June they left for the provincial capital of Bosnia: Sarajevo. But in Bosnia, agents of the Black Hand were waiting for them. On June 28th the royal couple went on tour of the city in an open car.
paragraphs.zh: 1908 年，奥匈帝国占领了波斯尼亚 和黑塞哥维那。 这激怒了隔壁的塞尔维亚人。 你知道，塞尔维亚民族主义者相信 波斯尼亚应当是更大的塞尔维亚 国家“大塞尔维亚”的一部分。 所以在这个时间前后，为了实现这 一目标而成立了一个塞尔维亚恐 怖组织。他们被称为“黑手”。 然后在 1914 年，奥匈帝国王储弗 朗茨·斐迪南大公决定与他的妻子 索菲前往波斯尼亚访问。 六月底，他们动身前往波斯尼亚的 省会：萨拉热窝。 但在波斯尼亚，黑手成员正等着他 们。 a 6 月 28 日，这对皇族夫妇坐在一辆 敞篷车上游览城市。

paragraphs.en: And as they drove through the city, a 19-year old agent of the black hand stepped forward and shot the crown prince and his wife, killing them both. Nobody could know it on that fateful June day, but Gavrilo Princip had just fired the first shots of World War One. But that is a story for next time. Okay everyone I hope you enjoyed that lesson and want to learn more. If so I recommend Extra Credit's video series on the assassination ofArchduke Franz Ferdinand called the Seminal Tragedy. As well as the Revolutions podcast and their series on the July Revolution of 1830 and the Revolutions of 1848. Thank you very much.
paragraphs.zh: 当他们穿过城市的时候，一名 19 岁的黑手成员冲上来向皇储和他 的妻子开了枪，将两个人都杀了。 在那个六月的致命一天没有人意 识到这一点，但加夫利罗·普林西 普打响了第一次世界大战的第一 枪。 不过这就是下节课的故事了。 好了，我希望大家喜欢这一课并且 希望了解更多。 如果的确如此，我向你们推荐 Extra Credit 关于弗朗茨·斐迪南 大公遇刺的系列视频。 以及 the Revolutions 博客和他们 有关 1830 年七月革命以及 1848 年 革命的系列。 谢谢大家。

titleZh: 第二十讲：第一次世界大战

paragraphs.en: Hello and welcome back to our lecture series on world history. Today we are going to learn about World War One. This lesson will focus on the years from 1914 to 1918. Here is what that looks like on our timeline. On June 28th, 1914, Gavrilo Princip assassinatedArchduke Franz Ferdinand ofAustria-Hungary and his wife, Sofia, while they were visiting Sarajevo. Princip was a Serbian nationalist who hated theAustro-Hungarian empire. By killing its crown prince, he hoped to strike a blow against it. Due to the smoldering aftermath of regional wars, Europe in 1914 was a gunpowder keg. This assassination was the spark that blew it up.
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到我们的世界历史 系列讲座。今天我们将要了解到的 是第一次世界大战。 这一课将聚焦于 1914 年到 1918 年 之间的时段。这是它在我们时间线 上的样子。 1914 年 6 月 28 日，加夫利罗·普 林西普在奥匈帝国弗朗茨·斐迪南 大公和他的妻子索菲访问萨拉热 窝期间刺杀了他们。 普林西普是一名仇恨奥匈帝国的 塞尔维亚民族主义者。他希望用杀 掉其王储的办法来打击奥匈帝国。 由于地区战争的后果持续郁积， 1914 年的欧洲成了一个火药桶。这 场刺杀则是引爆它的那个火星。

paragraphs.en: TheAustro-Hungarian empire was one the Central Powers, a network of alliances that also included Germany and the Ottoman Empire. Austria blamed Serbia for the assassination of its crown prince. Since Serbia belonged to its own global network, theAllied Powers, this quickly took on larger significance. Here are the two rival alliances that had become established by 1914. And here is a map of Europe at that time. Tan represents theAllied Powers, Pink represents the Central powers, and Green represents the neutral powers. The assassination of Franz Ferdinand brought these two alliances into confrontation. Aperiod of intense diplomacy and
paragraphs.zh: of 奥匈帝国是轴心国之一，它所在的 同盟关系网中还包括德国和奥斯 曼帝国在内。 奥地利因其王储的遇刺而指责塞 尔维亚。由于塞尔维亚也属于自己 的国际网络——同盟国，事情很快 闹大了。 这是到 1914 年已经建立起来的两 个敌对的联盟。 这是当时的欧洲地图。 褐色代表同盟国，粉色代表轴心 国，绿色代表中立国。 弗朗茨·斐迪南的遇刺导致这两个 联盟发生了对抗。 一个充满了紧张外交和谈判的时

paragraphs.en: negotiation began called the "July crisis." Once reassured of Germany's support, Austria-Hungary decided to blame Serbia for the assassination since Gavrilo Princip was a Serbian nationalist. On July 23rdAustria threatened Serbia with war unless it met 10 demands. The demands were extreme, but the Serbs were desperate to avoid war. They agreed to all of the demands but one. They refused to allow theAustrians to conduct an unrestricted police investigation, since that would mean the end of their sovereignty. Austria was not satisfied. On July 28th, declared war.
paragraphs.zh: 期开始了，这段时期被称为“七月 危机”。 在确认了德国的支持以后，奥匈帝 国决定为刺杀事件谴责塞尔维亚， 因为加夫利罗·普林西普是一个塞 尔维亚民族主义者。 7 月 23 日，奥地利威胁塞尔维亚， 如果不满足十个要求，就要向其开 战。 要求极为苛刻，但塞尔维亚人只想 避免战争。 他们同意了所有要求，只除了其中 一个。 他们拒绝允许奥地利人在境内开 展无约束的警力调查，因为这意味 着他们的主权完了。 it 奥地利感到不满。7 月 28 日，它向 塞尔维亚宣战。

paragraphs.en: Austria, it is widely thought, had wanted to conquer Serbia for a long time, and was looking for an excuse. The next day, Russia moved to protect Serbian ally and declared war onAustria. Russia had a huge army. Germany realized that once this army was mobilized it could be attacked as well. OnAugust 1st Germany declared war on Russia in support ofAustria. Germany worried about a two-front war with Russia and France. So onAugust 1st, Germany asked France if it would remain neutral or support Russia. When France’s response was ambivalent, Germany declared war on it the next day, August 3rd. Germany planned a two-front war against
paragraphs.zh: 普遍认为，奥地利是长期蓄谋要征 服塞尔维亚，一直在寻找借口。 第二天，俄罗斯出面保护其塞尔维 亚盟友，并向奥地利宣战。 俄罗斯有庞大的军队。德国意识 到，一旦这支军队被调动起来，它 也可能受到攻击。8 月 1 日，德国 宣布支持奥地利并向俄罗斯宣战。 德国担心会陷入与俄罗斯和法国 的双线作战。 所以在 8 月 1 日，德国询问法国它 是否会保持中立，或是支持俄罗 斯。 由于法国的回应模棱两可，德国于 是在第二天，也就是 8 月 3 日也向 法国宣战。 德国计划在“施里芬计划”下同时

paragraphs.en: FranceAND Russia under the "Schlieffen Plan." It was based on this principle: France was a smaller country than Russia, but its more advanced economy meant it could mobilize more quickly than Russia. Step 1 of the plan, therefore, was to send 80% of the army against France and 20% against Russia. The Germans planned to defeat France 6 weeks and capture Paris, as it had done in the Franco-Prussian war. After defeating France, Germany would turn its whole army east and defeat Russia. The Schlieffen plan, however, required the Germans to move through Belgium. When the Belgians refused to let the German army pass through their country,
paragraphs.zh: 与法国和俄罗斯双线作战。 这建立在这样的原则之上：比起俄 罗斯，法国面积较小，但它有更为 先进的经济，这意味着它能够比俄 罗斯更快地被调动起来。 因此，计划的第一步是将 80%的军 队派去攻打法国，20%去打俄罗斯。 德国人计划，要在六周之内击败法 国，占领巴黎，正如他们在普法战 争中已经做过的那样。 在击败法国以后，德国将调动它的 全部兵力向东，攻打俄罗斯。 然而，施里芬计划要求德国人横穿 比利时。 因为比利时人拒绝让德国军队通 过他们的国土，德国也向比利时宣

paragraphs.en: Germany declared war against them. German troops entered Belgium on August 4th. This violation of Belgian neutrality angered Britain, which had been trying stay out of the war. The Germans refused British demands leave Belgium, prompting Great Britain to declare war on Germany onAugust 4th. All of Europe had been pulled into a continent-wide war.And since these powers had colonies around the globe, would become a world war. InAugust 1914 the Germans’Schlieffen plan was proceeding. The French marched their army out to defend their country. French soldiers wore a uniform of blue
paragraphs.zh: 了战。 德军在 8 月 4 日进入比利时。 这一对比利时中立立场的侵犯激 to 怒了英国，此前它曾试图置身事 外。 to 德国人拒绝了英国对于他们离开 比利时的要求，导致英国在 8 月 4 日向德国宣战。 整个欧洲如今都被推进了一场洲 际战争。然后由于这些强国在全球 it 范围内拥有许多殖民地，这还将发 展成一场世界范围战争。 1914 年 8 月，德国的施里芬计划继 续推进。 法国人派出了自己的军队以保卫 国家。 法国士兵穿蓝色夹克和红色裤子

paragraphs.en: jackets and red pants. During World War One, 19th century ideas smashed into 20th century technology. This became obvious in first big battle between France and Germany: The Battle of the Frontiers. Like most, it dragged on for weeks instead of days. In that first month of fighting the French were still using 19th century methods of fighting, like a massed infantry charge. But it was no longer the 19th century. By 1914 all sides had high-powered rifles that had an effective range of 600 meters and could fire 10 shots before reloading. And World War One was the first large war with machine guns. And the first war with high-powered
paragraphs.zh: 的制服。 在一战期间，十九世纪的观念被融 进了二十世纪的技术。 这在法德之间的第一次重大战役 中变得显而易见，这就是边境战 役。 和大多数战役一样，它持续了数 周，而不是数天。 在第一个月的战斗当中，法国还在 使用十九世纪的战斗方式，比如步 兵的大规模冲锋。 但这已经不再是十九世纪了。到 1914 年，所有的参战方都装配有高 火力步枪，有效射程为 600 米，重 新装弹前可以射 10 次。 一战是第一场使用机枪的大规模 战争。 也是第一场应用高性能炸药火炮

paragraphs.en: explosive artillery. When the armies first clashed the generals didn't yet understand the power of these new weapons and a frightful slaughter ensued. Around a million men died in the first month of World War One. Against such weapons, ancient war tactics like the cavalry charge, bright uniforms and the bayonet charge became suicidal. Instead, a soldier's most valuable tools became the spade and pickaxe. Both sides learned the importance of digging in and fighting from trenches, not out in the open. After the first disastrous weeks, the French began to dig in. It was September and the German army
paragraphs.zh: 的战争。 当军队首次发生冲突时，将军们还 不了解这些新武器的威力，随后发 生了可怕的屠杀。 在一战的第一个月，大约有一百万 人死亡。 面对这样的武器，骑兵集结冲锋， 亮色制服和刺刀冲锋等古老的战 争战术变得与自杀无异。 相反，士兵最有价值的工具成了锹 和镐。 双方都学会了挖掘战壕和 在战壕中战斗的重要性，而不再在 开阔地带交战。 在最初灾难性的几个星期过后，法 国人开始挖掘战壕。 那是在九月，而德军已经距离巴黎

paragraphs.en: had made it to within miles of Paris. On September 12th, the two sides fought the Battle of the Marne. The dug-in French army fought desperately to defend their capital city. Fighting was so close to Paris, that Parisian taxis conveyed soldiers to the front. Unable to break each other’s lines, both sides began trying to outflank each other, stretching their armies north towards the English Channel. This part of the war, from September 17th to October 19th, is known as the Race to the Sea. By late October the German andAllied front line stretched 700 kilometers from Switzerland to the English Channel. The two armies fought along that entire
paragraphs.zh: 只剩数英里。 9月12日，双方爆发了马恩河战役。 已经挖好了战壕的法国军队拼死 捍卫他们的首都。 由于战斗地点距离巴黎如此之近， 巴黎出租车也运送士兵去往前线。 由于无法打破对方的防线，双方开 始试图相互包抄，将他们的军队向 北延伸到英吉利海峡。 从 9 月 17 日到 10 月 19 日，战争 的这个部分被称为海上竞赛。 到十月底，德国和同盟军的阵线已 经绵延 700 公里，从瑞士直到英吉 利海峡。 两军在整条阵线上作战。

paragraphs.en: line. The Germans had captured all of Belgium and Luxemburg and a good chunk of northeastern France, including most of France's coal fields. Now a bloody stalemate began that would last 4 years. Meanwhile things went differently along German's Eastern Front with Russia. The Eastern War began with Russia invading Germany inAugust 1914. But Russia was poorly prepared for war. In lateAugust 1914, the Germans won huge victory at the Battle of Tannenberg. Two Russian armies were destroyed and 92,000 soldiers became war prisoners. Asecond battle, in February 1915, was fought in the ice and snow of a Prussian winter.
paragraphs.zh: 德国人占领了整个比利时和卢森 堡，还有法国西北部很大一部分， 包括法国的大部分煤产区。 从这时开始，血腥的僵持将在未来 的四年持续下去。 与此同时，在德国与俄罗斯的东方 阵线，局势发生了很大变化。 俄罗斯入侵德国的东方战争开始 于 1914 年 8 月。 但俄罗斯对战争的准备很不充分。 1914 年 8 月底，德国人在坦能堡战 役中取得了一场大胜。 两支俄罗斯军队被摧毁，92000 名 士兵成为战俘。 1915年2月的第二场战役发生在普 鲁士冰天雪地的冬天。

paragraphs.en: The German victory here, at the Battle the Masaurian Lakes, stopped the Russian invasion. In the summer of 1915, the Germans and Austrians counterattacked in the Gorlice-Tarnow offensive. Here is the Eastern Front at the beginning of that offensive. Here it is at the end. Athird Russian army was destroyed and the Central Powers had advanced 300 kilometers into Russia. At this point the front line became static again. On both the Eastern and Western Fronts, trench warfare took over. Deep protective trenches were dug. Here is a diagram of a trench and an aerial view of the front line.
paragraphs.zh: of 德国在马祖尔湖战役中的胜利阻 止了俄军的进犯。 1915 年夏天，德国和奥地利在格尔 利斯-塔尔诺夫攻势中发动了反 击。 这是这次攻势开始时的东方战线。 这是其结束时。 第三支俄罗斯军队被摧毁了，轴心 国军力在俄罗斯境内向前推进了 300 公里。 此时前线再次进入僵持。 在东方和西方战线上，战壕战都变 成了主流。 深深的壕沟被发掘出来。 这是一 个沟槽图，以及前线的鸟瞰图。

paragraphs.en: Trenches were protected by another new weapon: barbed wire. Giant thickets were erected in front of trenches. It was almost impossible for soldiers to attack through the barbed wire. Many died trying. Opposing lines of trenches were separated by a deadly "no man’s land." Any soldier who tried to cross it was exposed to exploding shells and enemy bullets. Armies developed several lines of trenches: a frontal firing line, a support trench and a reserve trench. Soldiers rotated through these areas, since no one could survive in the trenches for too long without rest. For 3 years, millions of men lived,
paragraphs.zh: 壕沟被另一种新武器所保护：棘铁 丝。 巨大的铁丝丛林竖立在战壕前面。 士兵要穿过铁丝网发动攻击几乎 是不可能的。许多人在尝试通过时 丧命。 相对的铁丝网被一个致命的“无人 之地”分割开来。 任何试图穿越这一地区的士兵都 将暴露在炮火和敌人的子弹之下。 军队发展出了多层战壕：在前面的 火力线、起支持作用的战壕和备用 战壕。 士兵们在这些区域中轮换，因为没 有人可以在不休息的情况下在战 壕中存活太久。 三年里，数百万人像这样，在战壕

paragraphs.en: fought, and died in trenches like these. Sometimes the armies would try to attack by sending their men "over the top" and charging into no-man's land. However, attackers had little chance of making it through the artillery, rifle fire, machine gun fire, barbed wire and multiple lines of trenches. By 1915 this was the reality of the Western Front. In lateApril 1915, the Germans tried to break the stalemate at the Second Battle of Ypres. They became the first to use one of history's most horrible weapons. It was poison gas: Chlorine gas. When the wind was right, the Germans opened hundreds of canisters and sent blowing across no-man's land.
paragraphs.zh: 里生活，战斗，以及死亡。 有时军队会试图通过将自己的士 兵“派到上面”然后冲过无人区来 发起攻击。 然而，攻击手们很难通过火炮、步 枪、机枪、铁丝网和多重战壕。 到 1915 年，这成了西方战线的现 实。 1915 年 4 月底，德国人试图在第二 次伊珀尔战役中打破僵局。 他们成了一种名列史上最可怕之 一的武器的第一个使用者。 这是一种有毒气体：氯气。 在风向合适的时候，德国人打开数 it 百个罐子，让它随风吹过无人区。

paragraphs.en: French soldiers could either run or die. There was no way to resist the gas. The French position collapsed and the Germans advanced. Not even the Germans had predicted how effective the gas would be. TheAllied line was saved by the Canadian army fighting to the south of the French. At this point the Germans ran out of gas, so a regular battle began. The Canadians fought heroically and saved theAllies from disaster. Poisonous gas was only a super-weapon, however, in that one battle. Within weeks,Allied scientists had developed protective gas masks. Now the soldiers had an effective defense.
paragraphs.zh: 法国士兵要么跑、要么死。没有任 何方法能够抵御这种气体。 法国人的阵地崩溃了，德军继续前 进。 就连德国人也没能预测到这种气 体会有多么强大。 同盟军阵线被在法国南部作战的 加拿大军队拯救了。 在那时，德国人的氯气用完了，所 以一场常规战役开始了。 加拿大人英勇作战，让同盟国避免 了一场灾难。 然而，有毒气体只是这样一场战役 中的一种超级武器。 在几周之内，同盟国的科学家们就 研制出了防毒面具。 现在，士兵们有了有效的防护。

paragraphs.en: New and more terrible forms of gas were developed. While not tactically effective, they caused terrible suffering. Sometimes soldiers would be blinded by inhaling tiny amount of the gas. For example, these soldiers in this picture have all been wounded in a gas attack. The same situation is illustrated in this famous painting by John Singer Sargent. In 1916, both sides attempted to break through the trenches in two gigantic battles. The German’s launched an ultimately failed assault on Verdun, from February until December 1916. This was probably the biggest battle of the war. To help the French, the British launched counter attack on July 1st at the Battle
paragraphs.zh: 新的、更加可怕的气体被研发出 来，尽管没有战术上的有效性，但 它们造成了可怕的痛苦。 有些时候士兵会因为吸入了少量 有毒气体而致盲。 比如说，图上这些士兵都是在一次 毒气攻击中受伤的。 John Singer Sargent 这幅著名画 作中也描绘了同样的情形。 1916 年，双方试图在两次重大战役 中突破战壕的限制。 德国人在 1916 年 2 月至 12 月期间 对凡尔登进行了最终失败的攻击。 这可能是这场战争中最大的战役。 为了帮助法国人，英国人在 7 月 1 日的索姆河战役中发动了反击。

paragraphs.en: the Somme. Troops made repeated, brave, attempts cross the no-man's land that summer. The British introduced a new weapon- the tank- which was specifically designed to break through trenches. But the British did not understand how use them tactically and the attack failed. 2 million men died in the gigantic battles of Verdun and the Somme. And the war was only half-way over. Generals valued another new invention, the airplane. It allowed them to look down on the position of the opposing side. In response, the enemy began sending their own airplanes to shoot down these reconnaissance planes. This led to the first air battles in history.
paragraphs.zh: to 那个夏天，军队反复地对无人区发 起英勇冲击，试图通过。 英国人引入了一种新的武器—— 坦克。它就是为了突破战壕设计 的。 to 但英国人没有理解如何战术性地 应用它们，最终，这次反击失败了。 在凡尔登和索姆河两次重大战役 当中，有两百万人死亡。 而战争刚刚过半。 将军们也看重另一个新发明，这就 是飞机。它使得他们可以看清敌方 的阵地。 up 结果是敌人开始派遣自己的飞机 升空，为了击落这些侦察机。 这带来了史上的第一次空中战役。

paragraphs.en: The airplane was only 12 years old at this point—still a very new technology. Amajor part of the war was economic: which country would have the naval power to control the seas. In 1914, Britain had the world's biggest navy, followed by that of Germany. On May 30th, 1916, the Germans made one serious effort to defeat the British navy. The two battleship fleets sailed out against each other in the Battle of Jutland. It was the greatest battleship battle in history. The British won. The German navy made no further attempt to challenge the British directly at sea. With control of the sea, Britain could
paragraphs.zh: 在当时，飞机的研制还只有十二年 历史，仍是一个非常新的技术。 战争的一个主要组成部分是经济 战：哪个国家有足够的海军力量控 制海上。 1914 年，英国有全世界最庞大的海 军，紧随其后的则是德国。 1916 年 5 月 30 日，德国对英国海 军发动了一次重要攻势。 两支战舰编队在日德兰战役中发 生战斗。 这是史上最大的海战。 英国人赢了。德国海军再也没有在 海上直接挑战过英国。 有了海上控制权，英国人现在可以

paragraphs.en: now blockade German ports and cut off trade lines. This led to food shortages and bread rationing in Germany. Since soldiers were fed first, German civilians suffered the most. Especially the "Turnip Winter" of 1916 to 1917 when the potato harvest failed. About 400,000 Germans starved to death during the war. The German navy, however, did have one advantage. It was the world leader in submarine technology: another new invention. At the start of the war the Germans had 23 battleships, but 48 submarines.And they built a total 373 before the war was over. On February 4th, 1915, Germany
paragraphs.zh: 封锁德国港口并切断其贸易线。 这导致了德国的食品短缺和面包 配给制。 因为士兵们会得到优先供应，德国 in 平民因此遭受了最多痛苦，特别是 在 1916-1917 年的“芜菁之冬”， 当时土豆发生了歉收。 战争期间，有大约 40 万德国人饿 死。 然而，德国海军取得了一项进展。 它成了潜水艇技术的全球领袖，这 是又一项新的发明。 在战争开始时，德国人共有 23 条 战船，但还有 48 艘潜水艇。在战 争结束之前，他们一共建造了 373 艘。 1915 年 2 月 4 日，德国宣布建立一

paragraphs.en: announced a blockade of Britain and France by creating an exclusion zone where it would attack merchant shipping. In February 1915, German submarines began attacking trade ships. The most famous attack sunk the RMS Lusitania on May 7th, 1915. This attack outraged the world because the British ocean liner, coming from New York, was a passenger ship. But it was also secretly carrying weapons. 1198 passenger died in the attack including 128American citizens. Americans saw the attack as barbaric and cowardly. Public opinion turned against Germany. TheAmericans warned the Germans to stop the submarine attacks.
paragraphs.zh: 个禁止商船运输的禁区，以此封锁 英国和法国。 1915 年 2 月，德国潜水艇开始攻击 商船。 其中最著名的攻击事件是1915年5 月 7 日 RMS Lusitania 号的沉没。 这次攻击行动激怒了全世界，因为 这艘从纽约出发的英国远洋轮船， 是一艘客船。 但是，它也秘密携带了武器。 1198 名乘客在这次攻击中死亡，其 中包括 128 名美国公民。 美国人将这次攻击视为野蛮的懦 夫行径。公共舆论开始反对德国。 美国人警告德国人停止潜水艇攻 击。

paragraphs.en: Since the Germans wanted to keep America out of the war, they agreed. In 1917 the war entered its final phase. By 1917 and 1918, a serious famine gripped Belgium, Western Russia, Serbia and Romania. Italy,Austro-Hungary, the rest of the Balkans and the rest of Russia were close to famine. Food shortages were severe in France, Germany, and Turkey. Only theAmerican and British economies came through the war okay. The continental powers all saw their economies shrink by 20-40%. In Russia famine killed 700,000 people. And 2 million Russian soldiers died in the war.
paragraphs.zh: 由于德国人希望美国不要参战，他 们同意了。 1917 年，战争进入了最后阶段。 1917 年到 1918 年，比利时，俄罗 斯西部，塞尔维亚和罗马尼亚陷入 严重饥荒。 意大利，奥匈帝国，其他巴尔干地 区和俄罗斯其他地区接近饥荒。 在法国、德国和土耳其，食物短缺 也很严重。 只有美国和英国经济在经历了战 争以后还算不错。 大 陆 强 国 的 经 济 都 萎 缩 了 在俄罗斯，饥荒杀死了 70 万人。 还有两百万俄罗斯士兵死于战争。

paragraphs.en: By March of 1917 the Russians had suffered enough. Protests against the czar erupted across the country. On March 16th, Czar Nicholas II was overthrown by a Democratic movement led byAlexander Kerensky. Kerensky formed a new democratic government called "The Russian Provisional Government" in March. Kerensky decided to keep Russia in the war. In response, the Germans sent Russian revolutionaries, mostly Communists led by Vladimir Lenin, into Russia to cause trouble for the new government. At the time Lenin was living in Switzerland. On April 16th 1917, the Germans sent him and 31 other dissidents into Russia on a special train.
paragraphs.zh: 1917 年 3 月，俄罗斯人受够了。反 对沙皇的抗议在全国爆发。 3 月 16 日，沙皇尼古拉二世被亚历 山大·克伦斯基所领导的民主运动 推翻。 克伦斯基在三月成立了一个叫做 “俄罗斯临时政府”的新民主政 府。 克伦斯基决定让俄罗斯继续参战。 作为回应，德国人派出了俄罗斯革 命党，主要是由弗拉基米尔·列宁 领导的共产党人进入俄罗斯，为新 政府制造麻烦。 那时列宁住在瑞士。1917 年 4 月 16 日，德国人把他和另外 31 个异 见者用一列特别列车送进了俄罗 斯。

paragraphs.en: Around this time, however, the Germans made two mistakes that lost them the war. On February 1st, they announced the resumption of unrestricted submarine warfare. And on January 16th, the German foreign secretary,Arthur Zimmerman, proposed an alliance between Germany and Mexico. Zimmerman offered to help the Mexicans reconquer the territory they’d lost in the Mexican American war. He sent this proposal in a secret code. But the British intercepted the message, broke the code and gave it to the Americans on February 19th. The Zimmerman telegram, combined with the submarine attacks, caused America to declare war on Germany.
paragraphs.zh: 然而，正在此时，德国人犯下的两 个错误让他们输掉了战争。 2 月 1 日，他们宣布恢复无限制的 潜艇战。 在此前的 1 月 16 日，德国外交大 臣阿瑟·齐默曼提出德国可以与墨 西哥结盟。 齐默曼提出帮助墨西哥人重新征 服他们在美墨战争中失去的领土。 他用密码发送了这个提议。 但英国人窃听了这段消息，破译了 密码，并在 2 月 19 日将它转达给 了美国人。 齐默曼电报，加上潜艇战，导致了 美国向德国宣战。

paragraphs.en: So by the summer of 1917, the Americans were already sending money and troops to theAllies. But It would take America a year to build up a large force. Meanwhile the British and French made one more effort to break through at the Battle of Passchendaele in the second half of 1917. This horrific battle in the swamps of Flanders failed to end the stalemate. Meanwhile, Lenin had been busy in Russia organizing a Communist uprising. He rallied the people with the simple slogan of "peace, land, and bread." On November 7th, 1917, the Communists overthrew the government, forcing Kerensky into exile. On that day, the Soviet Union was born. Four months later, in March 1918, the
paragraphs.zh: 所以，到 1917 年夏天，美国人已 经开始向同盟国提供金钱和军力 支援。但建立一支强大的军队将花 费美国一年的时间。 与此同时，英国和法国在 1917 年 下半年再次尝试通过帕森德列战 役寻求突破。 这场发生在弗兰德沼泽中的可怕 战役并未能打破僵局。 与此同时，列宁正在俄罗斯忙于组 织一场共产主义起义。 他用简单的口号召集了人群，“和 平、土地和面包”。 1917 年 11 月 7 日，共产主义者推 翻了政府，迫使克伦斯基开始流 亡。 这一天，苏联诞生了。 四个月以后，1918 年 3 月，俄罗斯

paragraphs.en: Russians signed the peace treaty of Brest-Litovsk and left the war. Brest-Litovsk is one of the most unequal treaties in all of history. Basically the Russians surrendered all their land in Poland and Ukraine to Germany. And they agreed to provide Germany with supplies. Things got even worse for the Russians as a 5 year long civil war began between the Communists andAnti-Communists. So in 1917, the Russians were leaving Allies, but theAmericans were joining. The Germans tried to win the war before theAmericans arrived in large numbers, so they moved their Eastern army to the west. And then they launched the Spring
paragraphs.zh: 签署了布列斯特-利托夫斯克和 约，退出了战争。 布列斯特-利托夫斯克和约是史上 最不平等的条约之一。 俄罗斯人基本上把他们在波兰和 乌克兰的全部土地割让给了德国。 而且他们还同意向德国提供补给。 由于共产主义者和反共产主义者 之间长达五年的内战，俄罗斯人的 情况变得更糟。 the 所以在 1917 年，俄罗斯人离开了 同盟国，不过美国人加入了进来。 德国人试图在美国人大规模抵达 之前赢得战争，所以他们将自己的 东方部队调往了西方。 然后，他们在3月21日发动了1918

paragraphs.en: Offensive of 1918 on March 21st. With the eastern reinforcements the Germans were able to break through the trenches! The war became mobile again as the Germans pushed deep into France over the next 4 months. Again the Germans made it to the outskirts of Paris in the Second Battle of the Marne. TheAllies held out against the Germans in a desperate battle for Paris. In the end theAllies won. Now, it was all over for the Germans. Their best soldiers had led the attacks and almost all had been killed. And 2 million freshAmerican troops arrived in France. Untouched by war theAmerican soldiers
paragraphs.zh: 年春季攻势。 随着东部的增援，德国人如今能够 突破战壕了！ 随着德国人在接下来的四个月内 挺进了法国内部，战争再次变得机 动起来。 德国人在第二次马恩河战役中再 一次进入了巴黎郊区。 盟军为了巴黎，与德国人进行了殊 死搏斗。 最后同盟军赢了。 现在，对于德国人来说一切都完 了。他们最好的士兵参与了这次攻 势，而且几乎全部被杀死了。 同时，两百万美军有生力量抵达了 法国。 美国士兵没有卷入战争，他们新

paragraphs.en: were fresh, healthy, well equipped, and numerous. They tipped the balance of power. By August 1918, theAllies outnumbered the Germans. TheAllied counter-offensive began on August 8th, 1918. Over the next 100 days theAllies recaptured all the territory for which Germany had fought so hard in the Spring. Fighting was ferocious. But the Germans were simply overwhelmed. Their army and economy were collapsing. On November 11th, Germany surrendered. Seven months later, the treaty of Versailles ended the war on June 28th,
paragraphs.zh: 鲜、健康、装备精良，而且人数众 多。 他们打破了权力平衡。到 1918 年 8 月，同盟军人数已经远超德国人。 同盟军的反攻开始于1918年8月8 日。 在接下来的一百天里，同盟军重新 夺回了德国人在春天苦战夺得的 全部土地。 战斗十分惨烈。 但德国人已经不堪重负。他们的军 队和经济都在崩溃。 11 月 11 日，德国投降了。 七个月以后，在 1919 年 6 月 28 日， 凡尔赛和约结束了这场战争。

paragraphs.en: 1919. The war’s human cost was horrific; 19 million people dead, 23 million wounded. In political terms, it also had a huge effect. TheAustro-Hungarian empire broke up intoAustria, Hungary, Czechoslovakia, part of Romania, part of Poland, and Yugoslavia. The Ottoman empire lost control of its Arab territories, and became the country of Turkey. The Germans gave backAlsace and Lorrain to France, and lost some land in the east to the new country of Poland. And Russia turned into the Soviet Union. Here is Europe in 1914. And here it is in 1919.
paragraphs.zh: 这场战争造成的人命代价是令人 震骇的。有 1900 万人死亡，2300 万人受伤。 在政治角度，它同样造成了巨大影 响。 奥匈帝国分裂成了奥地利、匈牙 利、捷克斯洛伐克、一部分归了罗 马尼亚，一部分归了波兰，以及南 斯拉夫。 奥斯曼帝国失去了对其阿拉伯领 土的控制，成了今天的国家土耳 其。 德国将阿尔萨斯和洛林归还给了 法国，并在东方将部分土地割让给 了新波兰。 而俄罗斯变成了苏联。 这是 1914 年的欧洲。 这是 1919 年。

paragraphs.en: The boundaries of modern Europe were basically created by World War One. And finally, World War One set the stage for everything that would happen in the 20th century. But that story will have to wait for another time, because we have reached the 20th lesson in our series and the end of our history journey together. It has been an honor being your teacher, thank you very much. Finally, if you want to learn more about World War One, I have two recommendations for you. I recommend the classic novel All Quiet on the Western Front to give you a feeling for what being a soldier in that war felt like. And I recommend the Hardcore History
paragraphs.zh: 现代欧洲的疆界基本上是被一战 所确定的。 最后，一战为二十世纪将要发生的 所有事搭好了舞台。 但这个故事就必须等到其他时间 再来讲了，因为这是我们系列讲座 的第二十讲，也是我们历史之旅的 尽头。 很高兴能做你们的老师，非常感谢 你们。 最后，如果你们还希望了解更多关 于一战的事情，我有两个推荐。 我要推荐一下经典小说《西线无战 事》，它会让你体验到在这样一场 战争中做一名士兵是什么感觉。 我也推荐硬核历史系列播客：世界

paragraphs.en: podcast series: Blueprint forArmageddon by Dan Carlin. Its one of the best histories of World War One that I've ever heard. Thank you very much.
paragraphs.zh: 末日的蓝图，作者是 Dan Carlin。 这是我曾经听说过的关于一战历 史的最好作品之一。 谢谢你们。
